blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ I
DUALIZING COMPLEXESby A. Grothendieck
(written up by L. Illusie)
Introduction

This exposé is devoted to the biduality theorem in étale cohomology. The theory developed here and in SGA A XVIII (SGA A = SGA 4), for the étale topology and constructible torsion sheaves on preschemes, is formally very analogous to the one developed in HARTSHORNE [H] for the Zariski topology and coherent sheaves: the latter has, to a very large extent, served as its model. But, whereas in the case of "continuous" coefficients the existence of dualizing complexes presents no difficulties, in the case of "discrete" coefficients, on the other hand, it is much more delicate to prove, and it seems that it requires in an essential way the resolution of singularities. See however the biduality theorem of Deligne (SGA 41/2 Th. finitude 4.3.).
Paragraph 1 contains the definition of dualizing complexes and the formal properties which follow from it, notably the interpretation of dualizing functors as "exchangers of functors".
In paragraph 2, we show that, on a connected locally noetherian prescheme, a dualizing complex is determined uniquely, up to translation of degrees and tensoring by an invertible sheaf.

In paragraph 3, we come to the central theorem of the exposé (3.4.1.), which ensures that, on a "good" regular prescheme \(X\), for example an excellent noetherian regular prescheme of characteristic zero(1), the complex \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})_X\) is dualizing.

Paragraph 4 gives the applications of the theory to the local duality theorem. The latter expresses that we have, on a "good" prescheme \(X\) equipped with a closed point \(x\) with separably closed residue field, and for a constructible sheaf \(F\), a perfect pairing between \(\underline{H}^{\,i}(F')_x\) and \(\underline{H}^{-i}_x(F)\), where \(F'\) is the dual of \(F\), i.e. \(F'=R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,K_X)\), where \(K_X\) is a dualizing complex.
Finally, in paragraph 5, we prove directly that on a regular prescheme of dimension 1 the complex \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})_X\) is dualizing (\(n\) prime to the residue characteristics).

footnotes.html:
This last restriction will
become unnecessary once one has resolution of singularities à la
Hironaka for the latter, and the "purity theorem".

pages.title: 1. Definition and formal properties of dualizing complexes

blocks.html: 1. Definition and formal properties of dualizing complexes

We fix a coefficient ring \(A=\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\). If \(X\) is a prescheme, we denote by \(A_X\) the constant sheaf on \(X_{\mathrm{\acute et}}\) with value \(A\), and by \(D(X)\) the derived category of that of \(A_X\)-Modules. On the other hand we denote by \(D_c(X)\) the full triangulated subcategory of \(D(X)\) formed of the complexes \(F\) such that \(\underline{H}^{\,i}(F)\) is a constructible \(A_X\)-Module for every \(i\). We set \[D_c^*(X)=D^*(X)\cap D_c(X),\quad\text{where }*=\varnothing,+,-,\text{ or }b.\]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.1.

We say that a \(A_X\)-Module \(I\) is quasi-injective if we have \[\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,i}(F,I)=0\] for every constructible \(A_X\)-Module \(F\) and every \(i>0\).

Remarks 1.2. a) Contrary to what happens in the case of "continuous" coefficients (cf. [H] II.7.17), a quasi-injective sheaf is not in general injective. Take for example \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb{R})\), \(A=\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\): the sheaf \(A_X\) is quasi-injective, but is not injective, nor even of finite injective dimension, since \[H^*(X;A_X)=H^*(\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z};\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z})\] is a polynomial algebra on one generator of dimension 1.

b) Let \(I\) be a quasi-injective \(A_X\)-Module. It is false in general that the relation \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,i}(F,I)=0\) for \(i>0\), valid for \(F\) constructible, holds for every \(F\). Indeed, take \(A=\mathbb{F}_\ell\). Let \(k\) be a field, \(\bar{k}\) a separable closure of \(k\), \(f:\bar{X}=\operatorname{Spec}(\bar{k})\longrightarrow X=\operatorname{Spec}(k)\) the canonical morphism, and suppose that, for every connected étale covering \(X'\longrightarrow X\), there exists a connected étale covering \(X''\longrightarrow X'\) such that \([X'':X']\) is divisible by \(\ell\) (take for example \(k=\mathbb{Q}\)). Consider the exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow A_X\xrightarrow{\ \xi\ }f_*f^*A_X\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow0 \tag{*}\]

defined by the adjunction arrow \(\xi\). In view of the hypothesis, the section of \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,1}(F,A_X)\) defined by (*) does not vanish above any \(U\) étale over \(X\), hence \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,1}(F,A_X)_{\bar{x}}\ne0\). Now one verifies trivially that \(A_X\) is quasi-injective.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.3. (Cf. [H] 1.7.6)

Let \(X\) be a prescheme and \(N\in\mathbb{Z}\). The following conditions on \(K\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\) are equivalent:

(i) \(K\) is isomorphic, in \(D(X)\), to a complex \(I\) with bounded degrees such that \(I^i\) is quasi-injective for every \(i\) and zero for \(i>N\);

(ii) for every \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\) such that \(\underline{H}^{\,i}(F)=0\) for \(i<0\), we have \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,i}(F,K)=0\) for \(i>N\);

(iii) for every constructible \(A_X\)-Module \(F\), we have \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,i}(F,K)=0\) for \(i>N\).

Proof: (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii) Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\) be such that \(\underline{H}^{\,i}(F)=0\) for \(i<0\), and let \(I\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\) be with bounded degrees and such that \(I^i\) is quasi-injective for every \(i\) and zero for \(i>N\); let us show that \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,i}(F,I)=0\) for \(i>N\). Arguing by induction on the number of indices \(i\) such that \(I^i\ne0\), we reduce, by dévissage, to the case where \(N=0\) and \(I\) is concentrated in degree 0. We then have a biregular spectral sequence \[E_2^{pq}=\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,p}(\underline{H}^{-q}(F),I) \;\Longrightarrow\;\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,p+q}(F,I).\] Since \(I\) is quasi-injective, \(E_2^{pq}=0\) for \(p>0\) and every \(q\), hence we have \[E_2^{0i}=\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\underline{H}^{-i}(F),I) \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,i}(F,I),\] whence the conclusion.
(ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (iii) is trivial.

(iii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i) We may assume \(K\) with degrees bounded below and with injective components. The hypothesis, applied to \(F=A_X\), implies that \(\underline{H}^{\,i}(K)=0\) for \(i>N\). We may therefore replace \(K\) by an isomorphic complex \(K'\), with bounded degrees and such that \(K'^i\) is injective for \(i<N\) and zero for \(i>N\). One then observes that \(K'^N\) is quasi-injective, which completes the proof.
Remark 1.3.1. The writer does not know whether (1.3) is still true when, in condition (ii), the hypothesis "\(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\)" is replaced by "\(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(X)\)".

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.4.

We shall call quasi-injective dimension of \(K\), and denote by \(\dim.\mathrm{q.inj}(K)\), the lower bound (in \(\overline{\mathbb{R}}\)) of the integers \(N\) satisfying the equivalent conditions of (1.3).
If \(K\) is of finite quasi-injective dimension, the functor \[R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\ ,K):D(X)\longrightarrow D(X)\] induces a functor \(D_c^b(X)\longrightarrow D_c^b(X)\).

We shall see below that if \(X\) is smooth of dimension \(d\) over a field \(k\) and \(n\) is prime to the characteristic of \(k\), \(\dim.\mathrm{q.inj}(A_X)=2d\). The example of (1.2 a)) shows that a complex of finite quasi-injective dimension is not necessarily of finite injective dimension. We have however:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.5.

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme and \(K\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\). We suppose that there exists an integer \(N\) such that \(\operatorname{cd}_{\ell}(X)\leq N\), where \(\ell\) is the set of prime divisors of \(n\) (notation of SGA A X 1). Then, for \(N'\in\mathbb{Z}\), we have \[\dim.\mathrm{q.inj}(K)\leq N'\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \dim.\mathrm{inj}(K)\leq N+N'.\]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.5.1.

Let \(C\) be an abelian category satisfying (AB5) and possessing a family of generators \((U_i)_{i\in I}\). Let \(K\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(C)\) and \(N\in\mathbb{Z}\). The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(\dim.\mathrm{inj}(K)\leq N\);

(ii) for every \(i\in I\) and every quotient \(F\) of \(U_i\), we have \[\operatorname{Ext}^n(F,K)=0\quad\text{for }n>N.\]

Proof: It suffices to prove (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i). By a well-known argument (cf. for example [H] 1.7.6), we are reduced to showing that if \(K'\in\operatorname{ob}(C)\) is such that \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(F,K')=0\) whenever \(F\) is a quotient of some \(U_i\), then \(K'\) is injective. In other words, it is a matter of proving that, if every morphism from a subobject \(V\) of some \(U_i\) into \(K'\) extends to \(U_i\), then \(K'\) is injective. But this follows at once from the proof of Lemma 1 p. 136 of (Tohoku).

Proof of (1.5): Suppose \(\dim.\mathrm{q.inj}(K)\leq N'\) and let us prove \(\dim.\mathrm{inj}(K)\leq N+N'\). Since the category of \(A_X\)-Modules admits as generators the \(A_{U,X}\) where \(U\) is étale of finite type over \(X\), it suffices, by virtue of (1.5.1), to prove that \(\operatorname{Ext}^i(X;F,K)=0\) for \(i>N+N'\) when \(F\) is a quotient of such an \(A_{U,X}\), hence constructible (SGA A IX 2.9). Consider the spectral sequence \[E_2^{pq}=H^p(X;\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,q}(F,K)) \;\Longrightarrow\;\operatorname{Ext}^{p+q}(X;F,K).\] The hypothesis \(\operatorname{cd}_{\ell}(X)\leq N\) (resp. \(\dim.\mathrm{q.inj}(K)\leq N'\)) implies \(E_2^{pq}=0\) for \(p>N\) (resp. \(q>N'\)). Hence \(E_2^{pq}=0\) for \(p+q>N+N'\), whence the conclusion.

Remark 1.5.2. The hypothesis of (1.5) is satisfied (with \(N=2d\)) if \(X\) is a prescheme of finite type and of dimension \(d\) over a separably closed field \(k\), \(n\) being prime to \(\operatorname{car}(k)\) (SGA A X 4).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.6.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a separated quasi-finite morphism of noetherian preschemes, and \(K\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(Y)\). Then, for \(N\in\mathbb{Z}\), we have \[\dim.\mathrm{q.inj}(K)\leq N\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \dim.\mathrm{q.inj}(R^!f(K))\leq N.\]

Proof: By the "Main theorem" (EGA IV 8.12.6), there exists a factorization of \(f\) as \(f=uf'\), where \(f'\) is an open immersion and \(u\) a finite morphism. We have \(R^!f=R^!f'R^!u\), which reduces us to examining separately the case of an open immersion and of a finite morphism. If \(f\) is an open immersion, the assertion is trivial (since every constructible sheaf on \(X\) is then induced by a constructible sheaf on \(Y\)). Suppose therefore that \(f\) is a finite morphism, and let \(F\) be a constructible \(A_X\)-Module. We then have the duality isomorphisms (SGA A XVIII 3.1.9.6) \[f_*\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,i}(F,R^!f(K)) \xrightarrow{\sim}\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,i}(f_*F,K).\] Since \(f_*(F)\) is constructible (SGA A IX 2.14), it follows that, if \(\dim.\mathrm{q.inj}(K)\leq N\), we have \(f_*\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,i}(F,R^!f(K))=0\) for \(i>N\), hence \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,i}(F,R^!f(K))=0\) for \(i>N\) (SGA A VIII 5.5), qed.

Now let \(X\) be a prescheme, and \(K\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\). Denote by \(D_K\) the functor \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\ ,K)\). We shall define, for \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\), a functorial homomorphism \(F\longrightarrow D_KD_KF\) (cf. [H] V.1.2). The construction which follows would be valid more generally over a ringed topos. We may first assume \(K\) to consist of injectives, which allows us to omit the \(R\). The identity morphism \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,K)\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,K)\) defines, by the formula dear to Cartan, a homomorphism \[F\otimes\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,K)\longrightarrow K,\] whence, by a second application of the same, a homomorphism \[F\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,K),K),\] which is the announced homomorphism, and which we christen the canonical homomorphism. We say that the pair \((F,K)\) is bidualizing, or satisfies biduality, or again that \(F\) is reflexive relative to \(K\), if the canonical homomorphism \(F\longrightarrow D_KD_KF\) is an isomorphism.
From now on, all preschemes considered will, unless expressly stated otherwise, be assumed locally noetherian.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.7.

Let \(X\) be a prescheme. We say that a complex \(K\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\) is dualizing if the following conditions are satisfied:

(i) \(K\) is of finite quasi-injective dimension;

(ii) for every \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^-(X)\), we have \(D_K(F)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(X)\);

(iii) every \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\) is reflexive relative to \(K\).
Remarks 1.8. a) By a standard dévissage (truncate \(F\) and use the fact that \(D_c(X)\) is triangulated), one sees that condition (ii) is equivalent to
(ii bis): for every constructible \(A_X\)-Module \(F\) and every \(i\in\mathbb{Z}\), \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,i}(F,K)\) is constructible.

b) Condition (ii bis) implies \(K\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(X)\). Contrary to the example of coherent sheaves (EGA 0III 12.3.3), the converse is perhaps not automatically satisfied. It is, however (see n° 3), in the "good" cases, for example when one has on \(X\) resolution of singularities and purity (in particular if \(X\) is an excellent prescheme of characteristic zero).
c) By dévissage on \(F\), one sees that condition (iii) is equivalent to (iii bis): every constructible \(A_X\)-Module \(F\) is reflexive relative to \(K\).

d) If \(K\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\) satisfies (i) and (ii) (resp. is dualizing), the functor \(D_K\) induces a functor (resp. an equivalence of categories) \[(D_c^b(X))^\circ\longrightarrow D_c^b(X).\]

e) If \(K\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\) is of finite injective dimension (cf. 1.5.2) and satisfies (ii), then, for every \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c(X)\), we have \(D_K(F)\in D_c(X)\), and \(D_K\) induces a functor \[(D_c^+(X))^\circ\longrightarrow D_c^-(X).\] If moreover \(K\) is dualizing, every \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c(X)\) is reflexive relative to \(K\), and \(D_K\) induces equivalences of categories \[(D_c(X))^\circ\xrightarrow{\sim}D_c(X),\quad (D_c^-(X))^\circ\xrightarrow{\sim}D_c^+(X),\quad (D_c^+(X))^\circ\xrightarrow{\sim}D_c^-(X).\] We do not know whether this conclusion remains valid without the restriction that \(K\) be of finite injective dimension.
Let us now turn to the "exchange formulas".

We shall not return to the notion of tor-dimension of a complex (SGA A XVII 4.1.9)(*). Let us only note that if \(X\) is a prescheme and \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\), the relation \(\operatorname{Tor}_i(F,G)=0\) for \(i>N\) and every \(A_X\)-Module \(F\) is equivalent to the same with \(F\) constructible (thanks to SGA A IX 2.9 and to the commutation of \(\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}\) with inductive limits), so that there is no need to introduce a notion of "quasi-tor-dimension"!

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.9 (cf. [H] II 4.3)

a) Let \(X\) be a prescheme, \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c(X)\), \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\). We have \(F\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}G\in\operatorname{ob}D_c(X)\) if \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^-(X)\) or if \(G\) is of finite tor-dimension.

b) Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism of preschemes, and \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D_c(Y)\). Then, \(f^*(G)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c(X)\).

Proof: Assertion b) follows trivially from (SGA A IX 2.4). Let us prove a). Either of the hypotheses on \((F,G)\) implies that the spectral sequence \[E_2^{pq}=\sum_{q_1+q_2=q} \operatorname{Tor}_{-p}(\underline{H}^{\,q_1}(F),\underline{H}^{\,q_2}(G)) \;\Longrightarrow\; \underline{H}^{\,p+q}(F\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}G)\] is biregular. We are therefore reduced to proving a) when \(F\) and \(G\) are concentrated in degree 0, i.e. are constructible \(A_X\)-Modules. The question being local, we may assume \(X\) noetherian. Then \(F\) admits (SGA A IX 2.7) a left resolution \(F'\), where the \(F'^{-i}\) are of the form \(A_{U,X}\), with \(U\) étale and of finite type over \(X\). Now \(F\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}G\simeq F'\otimes G\), and we are done because the \(A_{U,X}\otimes G\) are constructible.

Notation 1.10. If \(X\) is a prescheme, we shall denote by \(D^b(X)_{\mathrm{torf}}\) (resp. \(D^b(X)_{\mathrm{qinjf}}\)) the full subcategory of \(D(X)\) formed of the objects of finite tor-dimension (resp. of finite quasi-injective dimension).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.11 (cf. [H] V 2.6)

Let \(X\) be a prescheme and \(K\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\).

a) For \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\) and \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\) (resp. \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\) and \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(X)_{\mathrm{torf}}\)), there exists a functorial isomorphism \[D_K(F\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}G) \xrightarrow{\sim}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,D_K(G)).\]

b) If \(K\) satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) of (1.7), we have the implication \[G\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)_{\mathrm{torf}} \Longrightarrow D_K(G)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)_{\mathrm{qinjf}}\] and \(D_K(G)\) satisfies (1.7. (ii)).

c) If \(K\) is dualizing, there exists, for \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^-(X)\) and \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\) (resp. \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\), \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\) and \(D_K(G)\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(X)_{\mathrm{torf}}\)), a functorial isomorphism \[D_K(F\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}D_K(G)) \xrightarrow{\sim}R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,G).\] We have moreover the implication \[D_K(G)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)_{\mathrm{torf}} \Longrightarrow G\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)_{\mathrm{qinjf}}\] and \(G\) satisfies (1.7. (ii)).

d) If \(K\) is dualizing and of finite injective dimension, the implications of b) and c) are equivalences.
Proof: a) is none other than the adjunction formula dear to Cartan (SGA 6 I 7.4).

b) Let \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)_{\mathrm{torf}}\). If \(F\) is a variable constructible \(A_X\)-Module, the cohomology objects of \(F\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}G\) are constructible (1.9), and vanish outside two fixed bounds independent of \(F\). Hence the same is true of the cohomology objects of \(D_K(F\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}G)\), and we are done thanks to a).
c) The first assertion follows from formula a) applied to \(D_K(G)\) instead of \(G\), and from the reflexivity of \(G\). The second is deduced from it by an argument analogous to b).

d) Let us prove for example the converse implication of b). Suppose that \(D_K(G)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)_{\mathrm{qinjf}}\) and that \(D_K(G)\) satisfies (1.7.(ii)). Then, for variable constructible \(F\), the cohomology objects of \(D_K(F\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}G)\) are constructible and vanish outside two fixed bounds. Hence the same is true of the cohomology objects of \(D_KD_K(F\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}G)\), but \[F\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}G \xrightarrow{\sim}D_KD_K(F\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}G)\] since \(K\) is dualizing and of finite injective dimension (1.8 d)), hence \(G\) is of finite tor-dimension. The converse implication of c) is proved in an analogous way.
Remarks 1.11.1. a) The writer does not know whether assertion d) of (1.11) holds under the sole hypothesis that \(K\) be dualizing.

b) One expresses the preceding proposition in a picturesque way by saying that the dualizing functor \(D_K\) exchanges the functors \(\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}\) and \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\), as well as the notions of finite tor-dimension and finite quasi-injective dimension, (the exchange being moreover fully satisfactory only when \(K\) is of finite injective dimension).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.12

Let \(Y\) be a noetherian prescheme, \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) a morphism of finite type, \(K_Y\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(Y)\). Suppose \(n\) prime to the residue characteristics of \(Y\), and set \(K_X=R^!f(K_Y)\) (SGA A XVIII 3.1), \(D_X=D_{K_X}\), \(D_Y=D_{K_Y}\).

a) There exists, for \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\), a functorial isomorphism \[\tag{i} Rf_*D_X(F)\xrightarrow{\sim}D_YR_!f(F).\]

If moreover \(K_X\) and \(K_Y\) are dualizing(1), there exists, for \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\), a functorial isomorphism \[\tag{ii} R_!fD_X(F)\xrightarrow{\sim}D_YRf_*(F).\]

b) There exists, for \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^-(Y)\), a functorial isomorphism(2) \[\tag{i} R^!fD_Y(F)\xrightarrow{\sim}D_Xf^*(F).\]

If moreover \(K_X\) and \(K_Y\) are dualizing(1), there exists, for \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(Y)\), a functorial isomorphism \[\tag{ii} f^*D_YF\xrightarrow{\sim}D_XR^!fF.\]
Proof: a) (i) is none other than the duality isomorphism (SGA A XVIII 3.1.9.6).

a) (ii) is obtained by applying \(D_Y\) to both sides of a) (i) written for the argument \(D_XF\), and using the "finiteness th." (SGA A XVII 5.3.6), which ensures that \(R_!f(D_X(F))\in\operatorname{ob}D_c(Y)\).
b) (i) is none other than the "induction formula" (SGA A XVIII 3.1.12.2).
b) (ii) is obtained by applying \(D_X\) to both sides of b) (i) written for the argument \(D_YF\), and using (1.9. b)).

Scholium: We may say, in a picturesque way, that the dualizing functors \(D_X\) and \(D_Y\) exchange the notions of usual and unusual direct (resp. inverse) image: \(Rf_*\) and \(R_!f\) (resp. \(f^*\) and \(R^!f\)).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.13

Under the hypotheses of 1.12, suppose moreover \(f\) proper, and let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\). If \((F,K_X)\) is bidualizing, \((Rf_*F,K_Y)\) is bidualizing. Conversely, if \(f\) is finite, and if \((Rf_*F,K_Y)\) is bidualizing, \((F,K_X)\) is bidualizing.

Proof: Suppose \((F,K_X)\) bidualizing. Applying \(Rf_*\) to the canonical isomorphism \(F\xrightarrow{\sim}D_X^2F\), we get: \[\begin{aligned} Rf_*F &\xrightarrow{\sim} Rf_*D_XD_XF\\ &\xrightarrow{\sim}D_YRf_*D_XF &&\text{(by a) (ii))}\\ &\xrightarrow{\sim}D_YD_YRf_*F &&\text{(by a) (i)).} \end{aligned}\] The courageous reader will convince himself that the isomorphism obtained is the canonical one, which proves (or will prove) the first assertion.

Suppose now \(f\) finite, and \((f_*F,K_Y)\) bidualizing (\(Rf_*=f_*\), \(f\) being finite), and let us prove that the canonical morphism \(F\longrightarrow D_X^2F\) is an isomorphism. By lemma 1.14 a) below, it suffices to show that the morphism deduced from it \(f_*F\longrightarrow f_*D_X^2F\) is an isomorphism. But, by a double application of a) (i), "one convinces oneself" that the latter is none other than the canonical isomorphism \(f_*F\longrightarrow D_Y^2f_*F\), which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.14

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a finite morphism of preschemes.

a) Let \(u:F\longrightarrow G\) be an arrow of \(D(X)\). For \(u\) to be an isomorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f_*u\) be one.

b) Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\). For \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c(X)\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(f_*F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c(Y)\).

Proof: a) The functor \(f_*\) being exact, we may restrict to the case where \(F\) and \(G\) are concentrated in degree zero. The assertion then follows at once from the computation of the stalks of \(f_*F\) (resp. \(f_*G\)) (SGA A VIII 5.5).

b) We may likewise restrict to the case where \(F\) is concentrated in degree zero. The necessity is known (SGA A IX 2.14); let us prove the sufficiency, i.e. show that \(f_*F\) constructible implies \(F\) constructible. The question being local (upstairs, hence a fortiori downstairs), we may assume \(Y\) (hence \(X\)) affine. There then exists (EGA IV 17.16.6) a finite family \((Y_i)\) of affine subpreschemes of \(Y\), pairwise disjoint and with union \(Y\), such that, if \(X_i=X\times_Y Y_i\), the induced morphism \(f_i:X_i\longrightarrow Y_i\) decomposes as \[X_i\xrightarrow{u_i}X'_i\xrightarrow{v_i}Y_i,\] where \(u_i\) is a finite radicial surjective morphism, and \(v_i\) a finite étale morphism. Set \(F|X_i=F_i\). By the base change theorem for a finite morphism (SGA A VIII 5.5), we have \[f_{i*}(F_i)\xrightarrow{\sim}(f_*F)|Y_i,\] and we are reduced (SGA A IX 2.8) to proving the assertion for \((f_i,F_i)\). Set \(F'_i=u_{i*}(F_i)\), so \(f_{i*}(F_i)=v_{i*}(F'_i)\). It is clear from the definition of constructibility (resp. follows from SGA A VIII 1.1) that \(F_i\) (resp. \(F'_i\)) is constructible if and only if \(F'_i\) (resp. \(v_{i*}(F'_i)\)) is, whence the desired conclusion.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.15

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a separated quasi-finite morphism of noetherian preschemes. Let \(K_Y\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(Y)\), and set, as in 1.12, \(K_X=R^!f(K_Y)\), \(D_X=D_{K_X}\), \(D_Y=D_{K_Y}\). Then, if \(K_Y\) is dualizing, \(K_X\) is dualizing.

Proof: We already know (1.5) that if \(K_Y\) is of finite quasi-injective dimension, the same is true of \(K_X\). Let us show that if \(K_Y\) satisfies condition (ii) (resp. (iii)) of (1.7), \(K_X\) satisfies it as well. By the "Main theorem" (EGA IV 8.12.6), \(f\) factors as \(f=f'i\), where \(i\) is an open immersion and \(f'\) a finite morphism. Since \(R^!f\simeq R^!i\,R^!f'\), we must therefore look separately at the case of an open immersion and of a finite morphism. If \(f\) is an open immersion, the assertion is trivial, seeing that a constructible sheaf on \(X\) is induced by a constructible sheaf on \(Y\), and that the formation of the \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\) commutes with restriction to an open subset. Suppose therefore \(f\) finite. We then know (1.13) that if \(K_Y\) satisfies (iii), so does \(K_X\) (taking into account the finiteness theorem SGA A IX 2.14). It remains to prove that if \(K_Y\) satisfies (ii), so does \(K_X\). Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^-(X)\); let us show that \(D_X(F)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(X)\). By the finiteness theorem, we have \(f_*(F)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^-(Y)\), hence, by hypothesis, \(D_Yf_*(F)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(Y)\). But \(D_Yf_*(F)\xrightarrow{\sim}f_*D_X(F)\) (1.12 a)(i)), and we are done thanks to (1.14 b)).

footnotes.html:
We shall see below that, under fairly general
conditions, \(K_Y\) dualizing implies \(K_X\)
dualizing.

If the functor \(R^!f\) is of
finite cohomological dimension, which is the case for example when
\(Y\) is of finite type over a field (SGA X 4.3 and XVIII 3.1.7),
the isomorphism b)(i) is valid for
\(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c(Y)\).

blocks.html: 2. Uniqueness of the dualizing complex
In this section, we assume \(A\) local, i.e. that \(A=\mathbb{Z}/\ell^\nu\mathbb{Z}\), \(\ell\) prime.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.1 (cf. [H] V 3.1)

Let \(X\) be a connected (locally noetherian) prescheme, and let \(K\) be a dualizing complex on \(X\) (1.6). Let on the other hand \(K'\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(X)\). For \(K'\) to be dualizing, it is necessary and sufficient that there exist an invertible \(A_X\)-Module \(L\) and an integer \(r\) (which are then determined up to unique isomorphism) such that: \[K'\simeq K\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}L[r].\]

Proof: Let \(L\) be an invertible \(A_X\)-Module, \(r\) an integer, and set \(L^\vee=\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L,A_X)\). There exist, for \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\) and \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\), canonical isomorphisms: \[\tag{*} R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}} (F\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}L^\vee[-r],G) \simeq R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}} (F,G\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}L[r]) \simeq R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,G) \mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}L[r].\] One deduces easily from this that, if \(K'\simeq K\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}L[r]\), \(K'\) is dualizing. Moreover, if we set \(D_K=D\), \(D_{K'}=D'\), we have, for \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\), \(D'F\simeq DF\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}L[r]\) by (*), hence, since \(DK\simeq A_X\), we have \[D'K\simeq L[r],\] which shows that \(L[r]\), hence \(L\) and \(r\), are determined in an essentially unique way by \(K'\).
Conversely, suppose \(K'\) dualizing, and set \[P=D'K=D'DA_X=R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(K,K').\] We are going to show that we have
a) \(K'\simeq K\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}P\),
b) \(P\simeq L[r]\), for an invertible \(A_X\)-Module \(L\), which will complete the proof of the theorem.

Let us first remark that by virtue of 1.9 b)(ii) we have, for \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c(X)\), \[\tag{**} F\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}P\simeq D'DF.\] Applying (**) to \(F=K\), we get \[K\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}P \simeq D'DK\simeq D'A_X\simeq K',\] which proves a).
Applying (**) to \(F=P'=DD'A_X\), we get \[P'\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}P =D'DDD'A_X\simeq D'D'A_X\simeq A_X.\] Hence b) will be a consequence of the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.2. (cf. [H] V 3.3)

Let \(X\) be a connected locally noetherian prescheme, and let \(P,P'\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^-(X)\) be such that \(P\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}P'\simeq A_X\). Then there exist an invertible \(A_X\)-Module \(L\) and an integer \(r\) such that \[P\simeq L[r],\qquad P'\simeq L^\vee[-r].\]
The proof will proceed in two steps.

1) Special case: \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(k)\), \(k\) a separably closed field. One need only copy the (moreover easy) proof given in [H] (loc. cit.). The important point is that (EGA 0I 5.4.3) if \(M\) and \(M'\) are two \(A\)-modules of finite type such that \(M\otimes M'\simeq A\), then \(M\) and \(M'\) are invertible and \(M'\simeq M^\vee\); it is here that the hypothesis \(A\) local intervenes.
2) General case.

By virtue of the preceding special case, there exists, for every \(x\in X\), an integer \(r(x)\) such that \[P_{\bar{x}}\simeq A_{\bar{x}}[-r(x)],\qquad P'_{\bar{x}}\simeq A_{\bar{x}}[r(x)],\] i.e. \(\underline{H}^{\,i}(P_{\bar{x}})=0\) (resp. \(\underline{H}^{\,i}(P'_{\bar{x}})=0\)) for \(i\ne-r(x)\) (resp. \(+r(x)\)), and \[\underline{H}^{-r(x)}(P_{\bar{x}})\simeq A_{\bar{x}},\qquad \underline{H}^{r(x)}(P'_{\bar{x}})\simeq A_{\bar{x}}.\] The point is to show that the function \(x\mapsto r(x)\) is locally constant. Indeed, suppose this point granted. Since \(X\) is connected, \(r\) is constant, say with value \(r_0\). We thus have \(\underline{H}^{\,i}(P)=0\) for \(i\ne-r_0\), \(\underline{H}^{\,i}(P')=0\) for \(i\ne r_0\), and \[\underline{H}^{-r_0}(P)\otimes\underline{H}^{r_0}(P')\simeq A.\] Set \(\underline{H}^{-r_0}(P)=L\), \(\underline{H}^{r_0}(P')=L'\).

Let \(u:\bar{x}\longrightarrow\bar{y}\) be a specialization arrow (SGA A VIII 7.2), \(u^*:L_{\bar{y}}\longrightarrow L_{\bar{x}}\), \(u'^*:L'_{\bar{y}}\longrightarrow L'_{\bar{x}}\), the corresponding specialization morphisms (SGA A VIII 7.7). We have a commutative diagram \[\xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{ L_{\bar{y}}\otimes L'_{\bar{y}} \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d]_{u^*\otimes u'^*} & A_{\bar{y}} \ar[d] \\ L_{\bar{x}}\otimes L'_{\bar{x}} \ar[r]^-{\sim} & A_{\bar{x}} . }\tag{*}\] Let us choose a basis \(e\) (resp. \(f\)) of \(L_{\bar{x}}\) (resp. \(L_{\bar{y}}\)) over \(A_{\bar{x}}\) (resp. \(A_{\bar{y}}\)), and denote by \(e'\) (resp. \(f'\)) the "dual" basis of \(L'_{\bar{x}}\) (resp. \(L'_{\bar{y}}\)), i.e. such that the image of \(e\otimes e'\) (resp. \(f\otimes f'\)) in \(A_{\bar{x}}\) (resp. \(A_{\bar{y}}\)) equals 1. We have \(u^*(f)=\lambda e\), \(u'^*(f')=\lambda'e'\), and the commutativity of (*) implies \(\lambda\lambda'=1\), hence \(u^*\) is an isomorphism (and \(u'^*\) the "contragredient" isomorphism). By virtue of (SGA A IX 2.13), this implies that \(L\) and \(L'\) are locally constant, hence of finite presentation as \(A_X\)-Modules. Consequently, if \(x\in X\), the canonical arrow \[\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(L,F)_{\bar{x}}\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(L_{\bar{x}},F_{\bar{x}})\] is an isomorphism for every \(A_X\)-Module \(F\). Since the geometric stalks of \(L\) are invertible, it follows that \(L\) is invertible. Likewise \(L'\) is invertible, and \(L'\simeq L^\vee\). Hence we have \[P\simeq L[-r_0],\qquad P'\simeq L^\vee[r_0].\]

Everything thus comes down to showing that the function \(r\) is locally constant. For \(m\in\mathbb{Z}\), we have \(r(x)=m\) if and only if \(\underline{H}^{\,m}(P')_{\bar{x}}\ne0\), hence, by virtue of (SGA A IX 2.4), \(r\) is locally constructible. Consequently, to check that it is locally constant, we may, by (EGA IV 1.10.1), assume \(X\) local. Let \(x\) be the closed point of \(X\), and let \(U\) be the complementary open subset.

We are going to show that \(r\) is constant. Arguing by induction on the dimension of \(X\), we may already assume that \(r(y)\) is constant and equal to \(r_0\) for \(y\in U\), and the point is to prove that \(r(x)=r_0\). Suppose this is not the case, i.e. that we have \(r(x)\ne r_0\), and let us show that we arrive at a contradiction. By hypothesis, we have \(P|U\simeq L[r_0]\), where \(L\) is an invertible \(A_U\)-Module. After translating the degrees if necessary, we may assume \(r_0=0\). On the other hand we have \(r(x)\ne0\), say \(r(x)=a>0\). Denote by \(i:U\longrightarrow X\), and \(j:x\longrightarrow X\) the canonical inclusions.

Let us write the exact sequence: \[\tag{**}0\longrightarrow i_!(P|U)\longrightarrow P \longrightarrow j_*j^*P\longrightarrow0.\] By hypothesis, the only nonzero cohomology objects of \(P\) are \(\underline{H}^{\,0}(P)\) and \(\underline{H}^{-a}(P)\), and we have \(\underline{H}^{\,0}(P)\simeq i_!(L)\). It follows that (**) splits naturally (thanks to the arrow \(P\longrightarrow\underline{H}^{\,0}(P)\)), hence that we have \[P\simeq i_!(P|U)\oplus j_*j^*P.\] From this we deduce \[\begin{aligned} P\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}P' &\simeq i_!(P|U)\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}P' \oplus j_*j^*P\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}P'\\ &\simeq i_!(P|U\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}P'|U) \oplus j_*(j^*P\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}j^*P')\\ &\simeq i_!(A_U)\oplus j_*(A_x), \end{aligned}\] which is absurd, since \(i_!(A_U)\oplus j_*(A_x)\) is manifestly not isomorphic to \(A_X\) (already the \(H^0\) are not isomorphic!).
This completes the proof of lemma 2.2.
3. Existence of dualizing complexes
3.1. Preliminaries

As was said in the introduction, the object of this paragraph is to prove that, on a "good" regular prescheme \(X\), the sheaf \(A_X\) is dualizing, \(n\) being assumed prime to the residue characteristics. Actually, one would like this to be the case on excellent regular preschemes of equal characteristics, but there is no doubt that the proof given below will extend to these once resolution of singularities and the purity theorem are available in that case.
We shall first present the various ingredients that we shall have to use.
3.1.1. Singular locus. Let \(X\) be a (locally noetherian) prescheme. We shall say that \(X\) satisfies condition (reg) if the following equivalent properties are satisfied (EGA IV 6.12.3):
(i) for every subprescheme \(Y\) of \(X\), the set \(\operatorname{Reg}(Y)\) of regular points of \(Y\) is open in \(Y\);
(ii) for every integral closed subprescheme \(Y\) of \(X\), \(\operatorname{Reg}(Y)\) contains a nonempty open subset of \(Y\).

Condition (reg) is satisfied if \(X\) is locally of finite type over a complete noetherian local ring (for example a field) (EGA IV 6.12.8), or over a Dedekind ring whose field of fractions is of \(\operatorname{car}.0\) (for example \(\mathbb{Z}\)) (EGA IV 6.12.6), more generally if \(X\) is excellent (EGA IV 7.8.6).

3.1.2. Dévissage of constructible sheaves. Let \(X\) be a noetherian prescheme, and let \(C\) be a strictly full subcategory of the category \(\operatorname{Cons}(X)\) of constructible \(A_X\)-Modules. We assume \(C\) stable under direct factors and extensions. The following conditions are equivalent:
(i) \(C=\operatorname{Cons}(X)\);
(ii) \(C\) contains the sheaves of the type \(i_!(F)\), where \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) is the immersion of an integral subprescheme, and \(F\) is locally constant on \(Y\);

(iii) \(C\) contains the sheaves of the type \(f_*i_!(A'_U)\), where \(i:U\longrightarrow Y\) is an open subset of an integral prescheme, \(f:Y\longrightarrow X\) is a finite morphism such that the generic point of \(Y\) is separable over its image and \(A'=\mathbb{Z}/\ell\mathbb{Z}\), for \(\ell\) a prime divisor of \(n\).
If \(X\) satisfies (reg) (3.1.1.), these conditions are also equivalent to the following:
(ii bis): analogous to (ii), \(Y\) being moreover regular;
(iii bis): analogous to (iii), with \(U\) regular.
If moreover \(X\) is affine, we may assume in (ii) (resp. (ii bis)) that \(Y\) is closed in an open subset of the type \(D(f)\), \(f\in\Gamma(X;\mathcal{O}_X)\).
Proof: The equivalence of (i)–(iii) was proved in (SGA A IX 2.5 and 5.8). The other assertions are deduced from it by noetherian induction.
3.1.3. Cohomological dimension. Let \(X\) be a (locally noetherian) prescheme. We shall denote by \((\mathrm{cdloc})_n\) the following condition:

For every subprescheme \(Y\) of \(X\), every geometric point \(\bar{y}\) of \(Y\), and every \(f\in\Gamma(\bar{Y}(\bar{y});\mathcal{O}_{\bar{Y},\bar{y}})\) (where \(\bar{Y}(\bar{y})\) denotes (cf. SGA A VIII 7.1.) the strict localization of \(Y\) at \(\bar{y}\)) we have \[\operatorname{cd}_{L}\bigl(\bar{Y}(\bar{y})-V(f)\bigr) \leq\dim\mathcal{O}_{\bar{Y},\bar{y}} \;(=\dim\mathcal{O}_{Y,y}\text{ by EGA IV 6.1.3}),\] where \(L\) is the set of prime numbers dividing \(n\).
Condition \((\mathrm{cdloc})_n\) is satisfied if \(X\) is locally of finite type over a field (SGA A XIV 3.5), or is an excellent prescheme of characteristic zero (SGA A XIX 6.2.)(*).

3.1.4. Absolute cohomological purity. Let \(X\) be a regular prescheme, and let \(Y\) be a regular closed subprescheme of \(X\) of codimension \(d\) at every point. We say that the pair \((X,Y)\) satisfies the absolute cohomological purity theorem (relative to \(n\)) if we have: \[\underline{H}^{\,i}_Y(A_X)=0\quad\text{for }i\ne2d,\] and if the "local fundamental class" homomorphism (SGA 41/2 Cycle 2.2) \[A_Y\longrightarrow \underline{H}^{\,2d}_Y\bigl((\mu_n)_X^{\otimes d}\bigr)\] is an isomorphism.
We say that the absolute (cohomological) purity theorem is true on \(X\) if every pair \((U,Y)\) as above satisfies it, where \(U\) is an open subset of \(X\).

The absolute purity theorem is true on \(X\) if \(X\) is smooth over a perfect field of char. prime to \(n\), (SGA A XVI 3.9), or is an excellent prescheme of characteristic zero (SGA A XIX 3.2), or is a regular prescheme of dimension 1 (cf. 5.1)(*).
3.1.5. Resolution of singularities à la Hironaka.

a) Let \(X\) be a regular prescheme, and let \(D\) be a \(\geq0\) divisor on \(X\). We say that \(D\) has strict normal crossings if there exists a finite family \((f_i)_{i\in I}\) of elements of \(\Gamma(X;\mathcal{O}_X)\) such that \(D=\sum_{i\in I}\operatorname{div}(f_i)\) (EGA IV 21) and that for every \(x\in\operatorname{Supp}(D)\) the \((f_i)_x\) such that \((f_i)_x\in\mathfrak{m}_x\) form part of a system of regular parameters. We say that \(D\) has normal crossings if \(D\) has, locally for the étale topology, strict normal crossings.
The typical example of a divisor with normal crossings is furnished by a locally finite union of coordinate hyperplanes in the standard affine space.

b) Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme. We shall say that \(X\) is strongly desingularizable if the following condition is satisfied: For every finite morphism \(f:Y\longrightarrow X\), where \(Y\) is integral and with generic point separable over its image, and every regular open subset \(U\) of \(Y\), there exists a proper birational morphism \(h:Y'\longrightarrow Y\), where \(Y'\) is regular, \(h\) induces an isomorphism of \(U'=h^{-1}(U)\) onto \(U\), and \(Y'-U'\) is a divisor with normal crossings.
Here are some cases where \(X\) is strongly desingularizable:
a) \(X\) is excellent, of characteristic zero ([2]);
b) \(X\) is locally noetherian, regular, and of dimension \(\leq1\);
c) \(X\) is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) or over a perfect field, and of dimension \(\leq2\) ([1])(*).

3.1.6. Cohomological finiteness. Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism of preschemes, and let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(X)\). We have \(Rf_*(F)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(Y)\) in the following cases:
a) \(f\) is proper and of finite presentation (SGA A XIV 1.1);
b) "when resolution of singularities and the purity theorem are available", in particular
(i) if \(X\) and \(Y\) are locally of finite type over a field \(k\) of characteristic 0 and \(f\) is a \(k\)-morphism of finite type (SGA A XVI 5.1);
(ii) if \(Y\) is excellent of characteristic zero and \(f\) of finite type (SGA A XIX 5.1);
(iii) if \(X\) is locally of finite type over a field and \(\dim(X)\leq2\) (SGA A XIX 5.1).
3.2. Quasi-injective dimension of \(A_X\)

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.2.1.

Let \(X\) be a regular prescheme, with residue characteristics prime to \(n\). We suppose that \(X\) satisfies conditions (reg) (3.1.1) and \((\mathrm{cdloc})_n\) (3.1.3) and that the absolute purity theorem is true on \(X\) (3.1.4). Then, for every constructible \(A_X\)-Module \(F\) and every \(x\in X\), we have \[\tag{*} \underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,i}(F,A_X)_{\bar{x}}=0 \quad\text{for }i>2\dim\mathcal{O}_{X,x}.\]

Proof: The question being local, we may assume \(X\) affine. By dévissage (3.1.2), we may restrict to checking (*) for \(F=i_!(G)\), where \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) is the immersion of an integral regular subprescheme closed in an open subset \(D(f)\) (\(f\in A(X)\)), and \(G\) is a locally constant constructible sheaf on \(Y\). We must compute \[\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,q}(F,A_X) =\underline{H}^{\,q}(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,A_X)).\] The projection formula for \(i\) (SGA A XVIII) gives \[R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(i_!(G),A_X) \simeq Ri_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(G,R^!iA_X).\] By the purity theorem, we have \[R^!i(A_X)\simeq(\mu_n)_Y^{\otimes-d}[-2d], \quad\text{where }d=\operatorname{codim}(Y,X).\] Set \((\mu_n)_Y^{\otimes-d}=T\). Since \(G\) is locally constant constructible, we have \[\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,p}(G,T)_{\bar{y}} \simeq\operatorname{Ext}^p(G_{\bar{y}},T_{\bar{y}})\] for every geometric point \(\bar{y}\) of \(Y\) and every \(p\). Now \(T_{\bar{y}}\) is injective, being isomorphic to \(A=\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\). Consequently \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,p}(G,T)=0\) for \(p\ne0\), hence \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(G,T)\simeq \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(G,T)\), whence finally \[R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,A_X) \simeq Ri_*(H)[-2d],\quad\text{with }H=\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(G,T).\] Let \(Z\) be the schematic closure of \(Y\) in \(X\) (EGA I 9.5.10). We have \(Y=Z\cap D(f)\) (EGA 0I 2.1.6). Denote by \(j:Y\longrightarrow Z\), \(k:Z\longrightarrow X\) the canonical immersions. We have \(Ri_*=k_*Rj_*\), so we are reduced to computing \(Rj_*(H)\). Let \(\bar{x}\) be a geometric point of \(Z\), \(\bar{Z}=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal{O}_{Z,\bar{x}})\) the strict localization of \(Z\) at \(\bar{x}\), \(\bar{f}\) the image of \(f\) in \(\mathcal{O}_{Z,\bar{x}}\), \(\bar{Y}=\bar{Z}\times_ZY=\bar{Z}-V(\bar{f})\), \(\bar{H}\) the inverse image of \(H\) on \(\bar{Y}\). By (SGA A VIII 5.2), we have \[R^qj_*(H)_{\bar{x}}\simeq H^q(\bar{Y},\bar{H}),\] whence \[\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,q}(F,A_X)_{\bar{x}} \simeq H^{q-2d}(\bar{Y},\bar{H}).\] Condition \((\mathrm{cdloc})_n\) then implies \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,q}(F,A_X)_{\bar{x}}=0\) for \(q-2d>\dim\mathcal{O}_{Z,x}\). From the relations \[d=\operatorname{codim}_x(Y,X)\leq\dim\mathcal{O}_{X,x} \quad\text{(EGA IV 5.1.3)}\] and \[\dim\mathcal{O}_{Z,x} =\dim\mathcal{O}_{X,x}-\operatorname{codim}_x(Z,X) \quad\text{(EGA 0}_{\mathrm{IV}}\text{ 16.5.12 and IV 5.1.9)},\] we deduce \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,q}(F,A_X)_{\bar{x}}=0\) for \(q>2\dim\mathcal{O}_{X,x}\), qed.

Remark 3.2.2. The bound obtained in (3.2.1) is the best possible. Indeed, let \(x\) be a point of \(X\) and \(Y\) the integral closed subprescheme of \(X\) having \(x\) as generic point. Since \(X\) satisfies condition (reg), there exists a regular open subset \(U\) of \(Y\) containing \(x\). And by the purity theorem, we have \[\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,2d}(A_U,A_X)|U \simeq(\mu_n)_U^{\otimes d},\quad \text{where }d=\dim\mathcal{O}_{X,x}=\operatorname{codim}(Y,X).\]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.2.3.

Let \(X\) be a regular prescheme of dimension \(d\). If \(X\) is smooth over a field \(k\) of char. prime to \(n\), or is excellent of characteristic zero, then \(X\) satisfies the conclusion of (3.2.1) and we consequently have \[\dim.\mathrm{q.inj}(A_X)\leq2d.\]

Proof: When \(X\) is excellent of char. 0, or when \(k\) is perfect of char. prime to \(n\), and \(X\) smooth over \(k\), the hypotheses of (3.2.1) are satisfied by virtue of (3.1.1), (3.1.3) and (3.1.4). The conclusion is still valid if \(X\) is smooth over an arbitrary field \(k\), as one sees at once by base change \(\operatorname{Spec}(k')\longrightarrow\operatorname{Spec}(k)\), where \(k'\) is a perfect closure of \(k\) (SGA A VIII 1.1).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.2.4.

Let \(X\) be a prescheme satisfying the conclusion of (3.2.1) and of dimension \(\leq d\). If there exists an integer \(N\) such that \(X\) is of cohomological dimension \(\leq N\), we have \[\dim.\mathrm{inj}(A_X)\leq2d+N.\] If moreover, for every closed subset \(Y\) of \(X\), \(\operatorname{codim}(Y,X)\geq p\) implies \(\operatorname{cd}_L(Y)\leq N-2p\), then we have \[\dim.\mathrm{inj}(A_X)\leq N.\]

Proof: The first assertion follows at once from (1.5). For the second, we argue as in (1.5). We are reduced to proving, for \(F\) constructible on \(X\), the relation \(\operatorname{Ext}^i(X;F,A_X)=0\) for \(i>N\). For this, we examine the spectral sequence \[E_2^{pq}=H^p(X;\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,q}(F,A_X)) \;\Longrightarrow\;\operatorname{Ext}^{p+q}(X;F,A_X).\] By (3.2.1), the sheaves \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,2i}(F,A_X)\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,2i-1}(F,A_X)\) are concentrated in codimension \(\geq i\). Hence \(E_2^{p,2i}=E_2^{p,2i-1}=0\) for \(p>N-2i\) by hypothesis. Hence \(E_2^{pq}=0\) for \(p+q>N\), whence the assertion.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.2.5.

If \(X\) is a smooth prescheme of dimension \(d\) over a separably closed field \(k\) of char. prime to \(n\), we have \(\dim.\mathrm{inj}(A_X)=2d\).

Proof: By (3.2.3) and (SGA A X 4.2), we are indeed within the conditions of application of (3.2.4) (note that the hypotheses of (SGA A X 4.2) are satisfied by virtue of (SGA A X 2.1 and 3.2)), which implies \(\dim.\mathrm{inj}A_X\leq2d\). The relation \(\dim.\mathrm{inj}(A_X)=2d\) comes from the purity theorem (cf. 3.2.2): if \(x\) is a closed point of codim. \(d\) of \(X\), we have indeed \[\operatorname{Ext}^{2d}(x,A_X;A_X) =H_x^{2d}(A_X)\simeq\mu_n^{\otimes d}(k)\ne0.\]
3.3. Constructibility of the \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,i}(F,A_X)\)

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.3.1

Let \(X\) be a prescheme.

a) The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) for \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^-(X)\) and \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(X)\), \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,G)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(X)\);

(i bis) for every pair \([F,G]\) of constructible \(A_X\)-Modules, \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,G)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(X)\), i.e. the \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,i}(F,G)\) are constructible for every \(i\);

(ii) for every open immersion \(i:U\longrightarrow X\) and every constructible \(A_U\)-Module \(F\), \(R^pi_*(F)\) is constructible for every \(p\);

(ii bis) for every immersion \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) and every constructible \(A_Y\)-Module \(F\), \(R^pi_*(F)\) is constructible for every \(p\);

(ii ter) for every immersion \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) and every locally constant constructible \(A_Y\)-Module \(F\), \(R^pi_*(F)\) is constructible for every \(p\);

(iii) for every finite morphism (resp. every closed immersion) \(f:Y\longrightarrow X\), and every \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(X)\), \(R^!f(F)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(Y)\).

b) These conditions are satisfied in the following two cases:

(i) \(\dim(X)\leq1\);

(ii) "resolution of singularities and purity are available on \(X\)" (cf. 3.1.6 b)).
Proof: The equivalence of (i) and (i bis) follows from the standard spectral sequence from the \(\operatorname{Ext}\) to the hyper \(\operatorname{Ext}\).

(i bis) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii). Let \(\bar{F}\) be a constructible \(A_X\)-Module extending \(F\), for example \(\bar{F}=i_!(F)\). By duality (SGA A XVIII 3.1.10), we have \[Ri_*(F)=Ri_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(A_U,i^*\bar{F}) \simeq R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(i_!(A_U),\bar{F}),\] and we are done.
(ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii bis). Factor \(i\) as a closed immersion followed by an open immersion.

(ii bis) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i bis). The question being local, we may assume \(X\) affine, and, by the usual dévissage (SGA A IX 2.5), we may restrict to checking (ii) for \(F=i_!(M)\), where \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) is the immersion of a subprescheme, and \(M\) is a locally constant (constructible) \(A_Y\)-Module. By the projection formula (SGA A XVIII) \[R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(i_!(M),G) \simeq Ri_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(M,Ri^!(G))\] and a spectral sequence, we are reduced to proving that \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(M,Ri^!(G)) \in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(Y)\).

Let us first show that \(Ri^!(G)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(Y)\). After factoring \(i\) if necessary, we may assume \(Y\) closed. Let \(j:U=X-Y\) be the complementary immersion. Applying the functor \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\ ,G)\) to the exact sequence \[0\longrightarrow A_{U,X}\longrightarrow A_X\longrightarrow A_Y \longrightarrow0\] we obtain a distinguished triangle \[\xymatrix@C=4em@R=2.5em{ & R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(A_{U,X},G) \ar[dl] & \\ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(A_X,G) \ar[rr] && R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(A_Y,G) \ar[ul] }\] Now we have \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(A_X,G)=G\). On the other hand, \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(A_{U,X},G)\simeq Rj_*j^!(G)\) belongs to \(D_c^+(X)\) by hypothesis. Hence \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(A_Y,G)\simeq i_*Ri^!(G)\) belongs to \(D_c^+(X)\). Resolving \(M\) on the left locally, for the étale topology, by free \(A_Y\)-Modules, we deduce from this, by a new dévissage, that \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(M,Ri^!(G)) \in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(Y)\), qed.
(ii bis) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii ter) is trivial.

(ii ter) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii). Let \(i:U\longrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, \(F\) a constructible \(A_U\)-Module. We must prove that \(Ri_*(F)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(X)\). The question being local, we may assume \(X\) noetherian, and we proceed by noetherian induction on \(X\). We may assume \(U\ne\varnothing\), so there exists a nonempty open subset \(j:V\longrightarrow U\) of \(U\) such that \(j^*(F)\) is locally constant (SGA A IX 2.4). Let \(M\) be the mapping-cylinder of \(F\longrightarrow Rj_*j^*(F)\). Applying \(Ri_*\), we thus obtain a distinguished triangle \[\xymatrix@C=4em@R=2.5em{ & Ri_*(M) \ar[dl]_{(+1)} & \\ Ri_*(F) \ar[rr] && Ri_*Rj_*j^*(F) \ar[ul] }\] Condition (ii ter) implies that we have \[Ri_*Rj_*j^*(F)\simeq R(ij)_*j^*(F)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(X).\] We consequently have \[Rj_*j^*(F)\simeq i^*R(ij)_*j^*(F)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(U).\] Hence \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(U)\), since \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(U)\). Moreover, the cohomology of \(M\) is supported in a closed subset \(Z\) of \(U\) distinct from \(U\). Applying the induction hypothesis to \(U\cap\bar{Z}\longrightarrow\bar{Z}\), we deduce that \(Ri_*(M)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(X)\), and consequently that \(Ri_*(F)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(X)\).
The equivalence of conditions (i)–(ii ter) with condition (iii) is left to the reader (use 1.14).
b) (i) By normalization, we reduce to the case where \(X\) is regular and we use purity (which will be proved in (5.1)).
3.4. The biduality theorem on regular preschemes

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.4.1. (cf. [H] V 2.2.)

Let \(X\) be a (locally noetherian) regular prescheme of finite dimension. We suppose that \(X\) is strongly desingularizable (3.1.5), satisfies conditions (reg) (3.1.1) and \((\mathrm{cdloc})_n\) (3.1.3), and that the absolute purity theorem (3.1.4) is true on every prescheme smooth over \(X\). Then \(A_X\) is dualizing (1.6).

Proof: We must prove that \(A_X\) satisfies conditions (i) to (iii) of (1.6). Condition (i) is satisfied by virtue of (3.2.1). Moreover \(A_X\) satisfies (ii) by virtue of (3.3.1 b)). There remains condition (iii). In other words, it remains to prove, by (1.7), that, for every constructible \(A_X\)-Module \(F\), the canonical homomorphism \(F\longrightarrow D_XD_XF\) (where we have set \(D_X=R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\ ,A_X)\)) is an isomorphism. The question being local, we may assume \(X\) noetherian, and by dévissage (3.1.2 (iii bis)), we may restrict to the \(F\) of the form \(F=f_*i_!(A'_U)\), where \(i:U\longrightarrow Y\) is the inclusion of a regular open subset in an integral prescheme, \(f:Y\longrightarrow X\) is a finite morphism such that the generic point of \(Y\) is separable over its image, \(A'=\mathbb{Z}/\ell\mathbb{Z}\), \(\ell\) a prime divisor of \(n\).

Since \(X\) is strongly desingularizable, there then exists (3.1.6) a proper birational morphism \(h:Y'\longrightarrow Y\), where \(Y'\) is regular, \(h\) induces an isomorphism of \(U'=h^{-1}(U)\) onto \(U\), and \(Y'-U'\) is a divisor with normal crossings (3.1.6). Thanks to the base change th. (cf. SGA A XVII) we have \[i_!(A'_U)\simeq Rh_*i'_!(A'_{U'}),\] where \(i'\) is the inclusion \(U'\longrightarrow Y'\), whence \[f_*i_!(A'_U)\simeq f_*Rh_*i'_!(A'_{U'}) \simeq Rg_*i'_!(A'_{U'}),\] with \(g=fh\). By virtue of (1.11), it suffices to prove that the pair

\((i'_!(A'_{U'}),R^!g(A_X))\) is bidualizing. Embedding \(Y'\) locally in a prescheme \(X'\) smooth over \(X\), and applying the purity theorem on \(X'\), we see that \(R^!g(A_X)\) is locally isomorphic to \(A_{Y'}\), up to translation. We are thus reduced to proving the following special case of (3.4.1):

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.4.2

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian regular prescheme on which the absolute purity theorem is true (3.1.4). Let \(Y=Y_1\cup\cdots\cup Y_p\) be a divisor with strict normal crossings on \(X\) (3.1.6), and let \(U=X-Y\). Then the pair \((A'_{U,X},A_X)\) is bidualizing (1.4).

Proof: Thanks to the exact sequence \[0\longrightarrow A'_{U,X}\longrightarrow A'_X \longrightarrow A'_Y\longrightarrow0,\] it amounts to the same to check that the pair \((A'_Y,A_X)\) is bidualizing. We argue by induction on \(p\).

a) \(p=1\). Denote by \(i\) the immersion \(Y\longrightarrow X\). By (1.11), it suffices to check that \((A'_Y,R^!i(A_X))\) is bidualizing. Now, by virtue of the purity theorem, \[R^!i(A_X)\simeq(\mu_n)_Y^{\otimes-1}[-2].\] Consequently, the canonical homomorphism \(A'_Y\longrightarrow D_YD_Y(A'_Y)\) is an isomorphism, as one checks trivially stalk by stalk (Pontryagin duality for \(A\)-modules).

b) \(p\geq2\). Set \(Y'=Y_1\cup\cdots\cup Y_{p-1}\). We have the exact sequence \[0\longrightarrow A'_{(Y-Y'),X}\longrightarrow A'_Y \longrightarrow A'_{Y'}\longrightarrow0.\] The pair \((A'_{Y'},A_X)\) being bidualizing by the induction hypothesis, we are reduced by the five lemma to checking biduality for \((A'_{(Y-Y'),X},A_X)\). Now, we again have an exact sequence: \[0\longrightarrow A'_{(Y-Y'),X}\longrightarrow A'_{Y_p} \longrightarrow A'_{Y_p\cap Y'}\longrightarrow0.\] Since \((A'_{Y_p},A_X)\) is bidualizing, we must therefore check that the same is true of \((A'_{Y_p\cap Y'},A_X)\). Applying (1.11) and purity once more, we are finally reduced to checking biduality for \((A'_{Y_p\cap Y'},A_{Y_p})\) on \(Y_p\), and we are done by the induction hypothesis.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.4.3

Let \(S\) be a noetherian regular excellent prescheme of characteristic zero. Then \(A_S\) is dualizing. Moreover, if \(f:X\longrightarrow S\) is a morphism of finite type, \(R^!f(A_S)\) is dualizing.

Proof: The first assertion follows from the fact that \(S\) satisfies the hypotheses of (3.4.1) (see 3.1). For the second, we must first check that \(R^!f(A_S)\) is of finite quasi-injective dimension. For this, we cover \(S\) by a finite number of affine open subsets \(S_i\) such that \(X_i=f^{-1}(S_i)\) is a finite union of affine open subsets \(X_{ij}\) embedded as closed subpreschemes in preschemes \(X'_{ij}\) smooth of finite dimension over \(S_i\). From (3.2.3) and (1.6), we then deduce that \[\dim.\mathrm{q.inj}(R^!f(A_S))\leq2\sup(\dim X'_{ij}).\] It remains to check conditions (ii) and (iii) of (1.7). Since these are of a local nature on \(X\) for the étale topology, we may assume that \(f\) factors as \(f=f'i\), where \(f':X'\longrightarrow S\) is a smooth morphism of relative dimension \(d\) and \(i:X\longrightarrow X'\) a closed immersion. We have \[R^!f(A_S)\simeq R^!i\,R^!f'(A_S).\] Now \(R^!f'(A_S)\) is locally isomorphic to \(A_{X'}\) (up to translation by \(2d\)), hence dualizing by virtue of the first assertion. Consequently, by virtue of (1.15), \(R^!f(A_S)\) is dualizing, hence in particular satisfies conditions (ii) and (iii) of (1.7), qed.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.4.4

Let \(k\) be a field, and let \(f:X\longrightarrow S=\operatorname{Spec}(k)\) be a morphism locally of finite type, with \(\dim(X)\leq2\). Then \(R^!f(A_S)\) is dualizing.

Proof: Replacing \(k\) if necessary by its perfect closure, a harmless operation for the étale topology (VIII 1.1), we may assume \(k\) perfect. We cover \(X\) by affine open subsets \(X_i\) of finite type over \(k\). Since \(\dim(X_i)\leq2\), \(X_i\) is finite over a \(X'_i\) smooth over \(S\) and of dimension \(\leq2\) (normalization lemma). By (1.15) we are then reduced to the case where \(f\) is smooth, hence \(R^!f(A_S)\simeq A_X[2d]\) (locally), i.e. to showing that in this case \(A_X\) is dualizing; but this follows from (3.4.1), since the hypotheses are indeed satisfied (see 3.1).
Remark 3.4.5. We shall see below that if \(X\) is regular of dimension \(\leq1\), \(A_X\) is dualizing.

footnotes.html:
(Added in 1977) or, by a
recent result of G. Ofer, if \(X\) is excellent, regular, and of
dimension 2.

(Added in 1977) more generally, if \(X\) is excellent noetherian of dimension \(\leq2\), by H. Hironaka, Desingularization of excellent surfaces, notes by B. Bennett, Bowdoin college, 1967 (to be supplemented if necessary by H. Hironaka, Certain numerical characters of singularities, J. of Math. of Kyoto Univ., 10, 1970, p. 151–187).

blocks.html: 4. Local duality
The object of this section is to present the notion of "biduality" developed in the preceding sections in a form closer to geometric intuition.

4.1. Let \(X\) be a prescheme, \(i:x\longrightarrow X\) a closed point with separably closed residue field, \(K_X\) a dualizing complex on \(X\) (1.7). We know (1.15) that \(K_{\{x\}}=R^!i(K_X)\) is a dualizing complex on \(x\), hence, by virtue of (2.1) and (3.4.4), isomorphic to \(A[r]\) for some \(r\in\mathbb{Z}\) (if \(A\) is local). We shall say that \(K_X\) is normalized at \(x\) if \(r=0\) and if we are given an isomorphism \[K_{\{x\}}\xrightarrow{\sim}A.\]

For example, if \(X\) is smooth of relative dimension \(d\) over the spectrum of a separably closed field, the complex \((\mu_n)_X^{\otimes d}[2d]\) is, by virtue of the purity theorem (SGA A XVI 3, SGA 41/2 Cycle 2.3), canonically normalized at every closed point of \(X\).

4.2. Let \(X\) be a prescheme, \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) a closed subprescheme, \(K_X\) a dualizing complex on \(X\). Then (1.15) \(K_Y=R^!i(K_X)\) is a dualizing complex on \(Y\), and we have the complementary induction formula (1.12. b) (ii)), for \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\), \[\tag{4.2.1}i^*D_X(F)\xrightarrow{\sim}D_YR^!i(F),\] where we have set \(D_X=R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\ ,K_X)\), \(D_Y=R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\ ,K_Y)\).

Let us apply (4.2.1) in particular to the case where \(Y=\{x\}\) is a closed point of \(X\) with separably closed residue field, \(K_X\) being normalized at \(x\) (4.1). Taking into account the fact that \(A_{\{x\}}\) is injective, (4.2.1) then reads \[\tag{4.2.2}D_X(F)_x\xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Hom}^{\bullet}(R^!i(F),A).\] Noting that \(R^!i(F)\) can also be written, by definition, \(R\Gamma_x(F)\), we thus have a perfect pairing in \(D(A)\): \[\tag{4.2.2}'D_X(F)_x\times R\Gamma_x(F)\longrightarrow A,\] giving rise to perfect pairings between finite groups: \[\tag{4.2.2}'' \underline{H}^{\,i}(D_X(F))_x\times \underline{H}^{-i}_x(F)\longrightarrow A.\]

For example, let \(X\) be a smooth prescheme of dimension \(d\) over a separably closed field, and let \(x\in X\) be a closed point. Then, for \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\), we have a canonical perfect duality between finite groups: \[\tag{4.2.2}''' \underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{\,2d-i} (F,(\mu_n)_X^{\otimes d})_x \times\underline{H}^{\,i}_x(F)\longrightarrow A.\]

Exercise 4.2.3. Let \(X\) be a strictly local scheme (SGA A VIII 4.2), with closed point \(x\), and let \(i:x\longrightarrow X\) be the canonical morphism. Let \(K\) be a dualizing complex on \(X\), normalized at \(x\). Denote by \[Li_?:D^b(A)\longrightarrow D^-(X)\] the functor defined by \[Li_?(M)=M\mathbin{\overset{L}{\otimes}}_A K.\] (If \(K\) is of finite tor-dimension over \(A\), \(Li_?\) extends to a functor from \(D^+(A)\) to \(D(X)\)).

Show that there exists, for \(L\in D^-(X)\) and \(M\in D^b(A)\), a canonical functorial isomorphism \[\tag{*}R\operatorname{Hom}(L,Li_?(M)) \xrightarrow{\sim}R\operatorname{Hom}(R^!i(L),M),\] (we assume of course that \(R^!i:D^-(X)\longrightarrow D^-(A)\) is defined ...).

Hint: First define a "trace" morphism: \(R^!i\,Li_?(M)\longrightarrow M\), then, to check that (*) is an isomorphism, reduce, by dévissage, to the case where \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\) and \(M=A\), and apply (4.2.2). Note that, for \(M=A\), (*) reduces to the perfect pairing ("local duality" cf. [H] V 6.2): \[R\Gamma_x(L)\times R\operatorname{Hom}(L,K)\longrightarrow A,\] or \[\underline{H}^{\,i}_x(L)\times \operatorname{Ext}^{-i}(L,K)\longrightarrow A.\]

4.3. We are now going to generalize (4.2.2) to the case of a geometric point \(\bar{x}\) of \(X\) localized at a point \(x\) which is not necessarily closed. For this we shall need some preliminaries.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.3.

Let \(X\) be a prescheme, \(X'\) the strict localization of \(X\) at a geometric point \(\bar{x}\), \(f:X'\longrightarrow X\) the canonical arrow. Then, for \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^-(X)\), \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\), the canonical functorial homomorphism (SGA VI I 3.7) \[f^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,G)\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f^*F,f^*G)\] is an isomorphism.

Proof: We reduce, as usual, to \(F\) and \(G\) concentrated in degree 0. The question being local in a neighborhood of \(x\), we may assume \(X\) affine. Then \(F\) admits (SGA A IX 2.7) a left resolution by sheaves of the type \(i_!(A_U)\), where \(i:U\longrightarrow X\) is étale of finite presentation, so, by dévissage, we are reduced to \(F=i_!(A_U)\). Let us form the cartesian square \[\xymatrix@C=4em@R=2.5em{ U' \ar[r]^g \ar[d]_{i'} & U \ar[d]^i \\ X' \ar[r]_f & X . }\] We have canonical isomorphisms, trivial special cases of duality (SGA A XVIII 3.1.10): \[R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,G)\simeq Ri_*i^*G,\] \[R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f^*F,f^*G) \simeq Ri'_*g^*i^*G.\] Via these isomorphisms the canonical arrow \[f^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,G)\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(f^*F,f^*G)\] is identified with the base change arrow (SGA A XVII) \[f^*Ri_*i^*(G)\longrightarrow Ri'_*g^*i^*(G).\] Now the latter is an isomorphism, as one sees at once by passage to the limit (SGA A VII 5.11).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.4.

Let \(X\) be a prescheme, and let \(K\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^+(X)\) be a complex satisfying conditions (i) and (ii) of (1.7). For \(K\) to be dualizing, it is necessary and sufficient that, for every strict localization \(f:\bar{X}(\bar{x})\longrightarrow X\), \(f^*K\) be dualizing.

Proof: Use the fact that every constructible sheaf on a strict localization \(\bar{X}(\bar{x})\) is induced by a constructible sheaf on an étale \(\bar{x}\)-pointed scheme over \(X\) (SGA A IX 2.7.4) and apply (4.3).
4.5. Let \(X\) be a prescheme, \(\bar{x}\) a geometric point of \(X\), \(f:X'=\bar{X}(\bar{x})\longrightarrow X\) the corresponding strict localization.

Let \(Y\) be the integral closed subprescheme which is the closure of \(x\) in \(X\), denote by \(g:\bar{x}\longrightarrow Y\) and \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) the canonical arrows, and form the cartesian square \[\xymatrix@C=4em@R=2.5em{ \bar{x} \ar[r]^{i'} \ar[d]_g & X' \ar[d]^f \\ Y \ar[r]_i & X . }\] (\(\bar{x}\) is at the same time the closed point of \(X'\) and the strict localization of \(Y\) at \(\bar{x}\)). We shall denote by \[R\Gamma_{\bar{x}}:D^+(X)\longrightarrow D^+(\bar{x})\] the functor defined by \[\tag{4.5.1}R\Gamma_{\bar{x}}=g^*R^!i.\]

Using the well-known relation between the functors \(R^!i\) and \(Rj_*j^*\), where \(j:U\longrightarrow X\) is the open complement of \(Y\), and applying passage to the limit (SGA A VII 5.11), we find a canonical isomorphism \[\tag{4.5.2}R\Gamma_{\bar{x}}\xrightarrow{\sim}R^!i'f^*.\]
When \(x\) is a closed point with separably closed residue field, the functor \(R\Gamma_{\bar{x}}\) coincides with the usual functor \(R\Gamma_x\) (SGA V 4.3), which justifies the notation.

Let \(K_X\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\), and set \(K_Y=R^!i(K_X)\), \(K_{X'}=f^*K_X\), \(K_{\bar{x}}=R\Gamma_{\bar{x}}(K_X)\) (warning: \(K_{\bar{x}}\) is not the stalk of \(K_X\) at \(\bar{x}\)!). Suppose that \(K_X\) is dualizing. Then, by virtue of (1.15) and (4.4), the same is true of \(K_{X'}\), \(K_Y\), \(K_{\bar{x}}\). Hence (2.1) we have \(K_{\bar{x}}\simeq A[d]\) for some \(d\in\mathbb{Z}\). We shall say that \(K_X\) is normalized at \(\bar{x}\) if \(d=0\) and if we have chosen an isomorphism \(K_{\bar{x}}\xrightarrow{\sim}A\). In the case where \(x\) is closed with separably closed residue field, we recover the notion introduced in (4.1).

Let \(K_X\) be a dualizing complex, and set \(D_X=R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\ ,K_X)\), \(D_Y=R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\ ,K_Y)\), etc. Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\), and let us compute \((D_XF)_{\bar{x}}\). We have: \[\begin{aligned} (D_XF)_{\bar{x}}&\simeq g^*i^*D_XF\\ &\xrightarrow{\sim}g^*D_YR^!iF &&\text{(by the complementary induction formula (4.2.1))}\\ &\xrightarrow{\sim}D_{\bar{x}}g^*R^!i(F) &&\text{(by (4.3)),} \end{aligned}\] that is \[\tag{4.5.3}(D_XF)_{\bar{x}} \xrightarrow{\sim}D_{\bar{x}}R\Gamma_{\bar{x}}(F).\]
Of course, we could have made an analogous computation by passing through
\(X'\) instead of \(Y\); we would then have obtained a canonical isomorphism \[(D_XF)_{\bar{x}}\xrightarrow{\sim} D_{\bar{x}}R^!i'f^*(F),\] compatible with (4.5.3) via the identification (4.5.2).

If moreover \(K_X\) is normalized at \(\bar{x}\), we obtain perfect pairings generalizing (4.2.2)' and (4.2.2)'': \[\tag{4.5.3}'D_X(F)_{\bar{x}}\times R\Gamma_{\bar{x}}(F)\longrightarrow A,\] \[\tag{4.5.3}'' \underline{H}^{\,i}(D_X(F))_{\bar{x}}\times \underline{H}^{-i}_{\bar{x}}(F)\longrightarrow A,\] (where we have set \(\underline{H}^{\,i}_{\bar{x}}= \underline{H}^{\,i}R\Gamma_{\bar{x}}\)).

Conversely, let \(K_X\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\) satisfy conditions (i) and (ii) of (1.7) and be such that \(K_{\bar{x}}=R\Gamma_{\bar{x}}(K_X)\) is dualizing for every geometric point \(\bar{x}\) of \(X\). Then, for every \(\bar{x}\), we can define (cf. 1.12 b) (ii)) a canonical arrow \[\tag{4.5.3} D_X(F)_{\bar{x}}\longrightarrow D_{\bar{x}}R\Gamma_{\bar{x}}(F) \quad\text{for }F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X).\] If the latter is an isomorphism for every \(\bar{x}\) and every \(F\), then \(K_X\) is dualizing. Indeed, we must check that the canonical arrow \[F\longrightarrow D_XD_X(F)\] is an isomorphism for every \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\). Now, at an arbitrary geometric point \(\bar{x}\), we have \[\begin{aligned} F_{\bar{x}}&\xrightarrow{\sim} D_{\bar{x}}D_{\bar{x}}(F_{\bar{x}}) &&\text{(puisque \(K_{\bar{x}}\) est dualisant)}\\ &\xrightarrow{\sim} D_{\bar{x}}R\Gamma_{\bar{x}}D_X(F) &&\text{(by (4.3) and the induction formula 1.12 b) (i))}\\ &\xrightarrow{\sim} D_XD_X(F)_{\bar{x}} &&\text{(by hypothesis),} \end{aligned}\] hence, subject to a compatibility check, \((F,K_X)\) is bidualizing, which proves our assertion. To summarize:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.5.4.

Let \(X\) be a prescheme, and \(K_X\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\) a complex satisfying conditions (i) and (ii) of (1.7). For \(K_X\) to be dualizing, it is necessary and sufficient that for every geometric point \(\bar{x}\) of \(X\) the complex \(K_{\bar{x}}\) be dualizing and that the canonical arrow (4.5.3) be an isomorphism for every \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\).

Remark 4.5.5. The existence of dualizing complexes must be regarded, in a sense, as an important complement to the global duality theorem. Indeed, the latter expresses that we have, for a separated morphism of finite type \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) (\(Y\) being quasi-compact), a canonical isomorphism \[D_YR_!f(F)\xrightarrow{\sim}Rf_*D_X(F)\] (with the notations of 1.12).

Its exploitation for the computation of \(R_!f(F)\) (when \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\)) requires information about \(D_X(F)\). When \(K_X\) is dualizing, this is provided by the isomorphism (4.5.3), which allows, at least theoretically, a computation of the stalks of \(D_X(F)\) as "purely spatial" cohomology invariants.

Exercise 4.5.6. Let \(X\) be a prescheme equipped with a dualizing complex (1.7). The family of functors (cf. (4.5.3)'') \[\underline{H}^{\,i}_{\bar{x}}:D_c^b(X)\longrightarrow(A\text{-modules})\] (\(i\) running through \(\mathbb{Z}\) and \(\bar{x}\) through the set of geometric points of \(X\)) is "conservative", i.e. a morphism \(F\longrightarrow G\) in \(D_c^b(X)\) such that the \(\underline{H}^{\,i}_{\bar{x}}(F)\longrightarrow \underline{H}^{\,i}_{\bar{x}}(G)\) are isomorphisms, is an isomorphism.

4.6. Let \(X\) be a prescheme, \(j:Y\longrightarrow X\) a closed subprescheme, \(i:\bar{x}\longrightarrow Y\) a geometric point of \(Y\). Let on the other hand \(K_X\) be a dualizing complex on \(X\), normalized at \(\bar{x}\) (cf. (4.5)). We saw in (4.5) that, if \(Y\) is the closure of \(x\), we have, for \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\), a perfect pairing \[R^!j(F)_{\bar{x}}\times D_X(F)_{\bar{x}}\longrightarrow A.\] It is not difficult to define an analogous pairing in the general case. Let us introduce the factorization of \(i\) as \[\bar{x}\xrightarrow{i'}\{\bar{x}\}\xrightarrow{i''}Y.\] For \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\), we have canonical isomorphisms: \[\begin{aligned} R\Gamma_{\bar{x}}j^*D_X(F) &\xrightarrow{\sim}R\Gamma_{\bar{x}}D_YR^!j(F) &&\text{(complementary induction formula (4.2.1))}\\ &\xrightarrow{\sim}i'^*R^!i''D_YR^!j(F) &&\text{(définition de \(R\Gamma_{\bar{x}}\) (4.5.1))}\\ &\xrightarrow{\sim}i'^*D_{\bar{x}}R^!i''R^!j(F) &&\text{(ordinary induction (1.12. b) (i))}\\ &\xrightarrow{\sim}D_{\bar{x}}i'^*R^!j(F) &&\text{(by 4.3),} \end{aligned}\] i.e. finally a canonical isomorphism \[\tag{4.6.1}R^!j(F)_{\bar{x}}\xrightarrow{\sim} D_{\bar{x}}R\Gamma_{\bar{x}}j^*D_X(F),\] whence perfect pairings \[\tag{4.6.1}'R^!j(F)_{\bar{x}}\times R\Gamma_{\bar{x}}j^*D_X(F)\longrightarrow A,\] \[\tag{4.6.1}'' \underline{H}^{\,i}_Y(F)_{\bar{x}}\times \underline{H}^{-i}_{\bar{x}}(D_X(F)|Y)\longrightarrow A.\]

Example 4.6.2. Let \(X\) be a regular prescheme, \(\bar{x}\) a geometric point of \(X\), \(d=\dim(\mathcal{O}_{X,x})\). Set \(K_X=(\mu_n)_X^{\otimes d}[2d]\). If \(X\) satisfies condition (reg) (3.1.1) and the purity theorem (3.1.4), then \(K_X\) is canonically normalized at \(\bar{x}\). Hence, if \(K_X\) is dualizing, we have perfect pairings \[\underline{H}^{\,i}_Y(F)_{\bar{x}}\times \underline{H}^{\,2d-i}_{\bar{x}}(F'|Y)\longrightarrow A,\] for every closed subset \(Y\) containing \(x\) and every \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\), with \[F'=R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,(\mu_n)_X^{\otimes d}).\]
To finish, let us give a strictly local variant of (4.6.1).

4.7. Let \(X\) be a noetherian strictly local scheme (SGA A VIII 4.2), with closed point \(x\). Let \(Y\) be a nonempty closed subset of \(X\), and consider the canonical immersions \[U=X-Y\xrightarrow{i}V=X-\{x\}\xrightarrow{j}X.\] Finally, let \(K_X\) be a dualizing complex normalized at \(x\) (4.1), whence "corresponding" dualizing complexes on \(U,V,Y,x\): \[K_V=j^*K_X,\qquad K_U=i^*K_V,\qquad K_Y=R\Gamma_Y(K_X).\] Let \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(U)\), and set \(G'=D_U(G)\). We propose to define a natural pairing \[\tag{4.7.1}R\Gamma(U,G)\times R\Gamma(V,i_*G')[-1]\longrightarrow A,\] i.e. a functorial homomorphism

\[\tag{4.7.1}'R\Gamma(U,G)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes_A} R\Gamma(V,i_!G')[-1]\longrightarrow A.\] (There is a difficulty here: \(A=\mathbf Z/\ell^\nu\mathbf Z\) is of infinite cohomological dimension if \(\nu\geq2\), and a priori one only knows how to define the tensor products \[\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}:D^-(A)\times D^-(A)\longrightarrow D^-(A)\] and \[\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}:D(A)\times D^b(A)_{\mathrm{torf}} \longrightarrow D(A).\](*) We therefore leave to the reader the task of making suitable hypotheses ensuring that the two factors of \(\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}\) appearing in (4.7.1)' are in \(D^b(A)\), or that one of them is of finite tor-dimension.)

By complementary duality (1.12 a) (ii), we have an isomorphism \[\tag{4.7.2}i_!(G')\xrightarrow{\sim}D_V(Ri_*G).\] On the other hand, by ordinary duality, we have \[\tag{4.7.3}R\operatorname{Hom}(A_U,G)\xrightarrow{\sim} R\operatorname{Hom}(A_V,Ri_*G).\] By definition of \(D_V\), we have a canonical homomorphism \[\tag{4.7.4}Ri_*G\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}D_V(Ri_*G) \longrightarrow K_V.\] We thus deduce from (4.7.2), (4.7.3) and (4.7.4) a homomorphism \[\tag{4.7.5}R\Gamma(U,G)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes} R\Gamma(V,i_!G')\longrightarrow R\operatorname{Hom}(A_V,K_V).\] Since \(K_V=j^*K_X\), we have by duality \[\tag{4.7.6}R\operatorname{Hom}(A_V,K_V)\xrightarrow{\sim} R\operatorname{Hom}(A_{V,X},K_X).\] Finally, the exact sequence \[\tag{4.7.7}0\longrightarrow A_{V,X}\longrightarrow A_X \longrightarrow A_x\longrightarrow0\] gives a homomorphism of degree \(+1\) \[\tag{4.7.8}R\operatorname{Hom}(A_{V,X},K_X)\longrightarrow R\operatorname{Hom}(A_x,K_X)\simeq A.\] Composing, we obtain (4.7.1)'.

It remains to prove that this pairing is perfect. We are going to identify \(R\Gamma(V,i_!G')[-1]\) with \(D_A R\Gamma(U,G)\). Suppose first that \(G=(ji)^*F\), where \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\). Set \(F'=D_X(F)\) and \(F'_{U,X}=(ji)_!G'\). The exact sequence \[\tag{4.7.9}0\longrightarrow F'_{U,X}\longrightarrow F' \longrightarrow F'|Y\longrightarrow0\] gives rise to a distinguished triangle \[ \begin{array}{c} R\Gamma_x(F'_{U,X})\\[-2mm] \swarrow^{+1}\qquad\searrow\\[-2mm] R\Gamma_x(F'|Y)\longleftarrow R\Gamma_x(F'). \end{array}\tag{b} \] By definition, \[R\Gamma_x(F'_{U,X})=R\operatorname{Hom}(A_x,F'_{U,X}).\] Taking into account (4.7.7), and the fact that \((F'_{U,X})_x=R\Gamma(X,F'_{U,X})=0\), we have a canonical isomorphism \[R\operatorname{Hom}(A_{V,X},F'_{U,X})[1]\xrightarrow{\sim} R\Gamma_x(F'_{U,X}).\] But we have \[ \begin{aligned} R\operatorname{Hom}(A_{V,X},F'_{U,X}) &=R\operatorname{Hom}(j_!A_V,j_!i_!G')\\ &\simeq R\operatorname{Hom}(A_V,i_!G')\quad \text{by duality (SGA 3.1.10),} \end{aligned} \] whence finally a canonical isomorphism \[\tag{4.7.10}R\Gamma(V,i_!G')[-1]\xrightarrow{\sim} R\Gamma_x(F'_{U,X}).\]

On the other hand we have the exact sequence \[\tag{4.7.11}0\longrightarrow A_{U,X}\longrightarrow A_X \longrightarrow A_Y\longrightarrow0,\] which gives rise to a distinguished triangle \[ \begin{array}{c} R\Gamma(U,F|U)\\[-2mm] \swarrow^{+1}\qquad\nearrow\\[-2mm] R\Gamma_Y(F)\longrightarrow R\Gamma(X,F). \end{array}\tag{a} \] By virtue of (4.7.1)' and (4.7.10), we have a natural pairing \[\tag{4.7.12}R\Gamma(U,F|U)\times R\Gamma_x(F'_{U,X})\longrightarrow A.\] By virtue of (4.6.1)', we have a perfect pairing \[\tag{4.7.13}R\Gamma_Y(F)\times R\Gamma_x(F'|Y)\longrightarrow A.\] Finally, by the ordinary induction formula (1.12 b) (i), we have a perfect pairing \[\tag{4.7.14}R\Gamma(X,F)\times R\Gamma_x(F')\longrightarrow A.\] The reader who has the patience to find his way through this maze of "canonical arrows" will check that the pairings (4.7.12), (4.7.13), and (4.7.14) are "compatible" with the arrows of the triangles (a) and (b), and consequently that the pairings (4.7.12) and (4.7.1) are perfect.

The pairing of the triangles (a) and (b) gives rise to two exact sequences "transposed of each other": \[ \tag{4.7.15} \begin{aligned} \cdots&\longrightarrow H_Y^i(X,F)\longrightarrow H^i(X,F) \longrightarrow H^i(U,F)\longrightarrow\cdots\\ \cdots&\longleftarrow H_x^{-i}(Y,F'|Y)\longleftarrow H_x^{-i}(X,F')\longleftarrow H^{-i-1}(V,F'_{U,V}) \longleftarrow\cdots. \end{aligned} \]

Proposition 4.7.16. Let \(X\) be a noetherian strictly local scheme, with closed point \(x\). Let \(Y\) be a nonempty closed subset of \(X\), \(U=X-Y\), \(V=X-x\), \(i:U\longrightarrow V\) and \(j:V\longrightarrow X\) the canonical inclusions. Let \(K_X\) be a dualizing complex on \(X\) normalized at \(x\) (whence associated dualizing complexes on \(V,U,Y\)).

For \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(U)\), we have a perfect pairing: \[\tag{*}R\Gamma(U,G)\times R\Gamma(V,i_!G')[-1]\longrightarrow A\] (where \(G'=D_U(G)\)).

If \(G=F|U\), where \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\), we have a canonical isomorphism \[\tag{**}R\Gamma(V,i_!G')[-1]\xrightarrow{\sim} R\Gamma_x(F'_{U,X})\] (where \(F'=D_X(F)\) and \(F'_{U,X}=(ji)_!(F'|U)\simeq(ji)_!(G')\)), and a perfect pairing between the distinguished triangles \[ \begin{array}{c} R\Gamma(U,F|U)\\[-2mm] \swarrow^{+1}\qquad\nearrow\\[-2mm] R\Gamma_Y(F)\longrightarrow R\Gamma(X,F) \end{array} \qquad\text{and}\qquad \begin{array}{c} R\Gamma_x(F'_{U,X})\\[-2mm] \nearrow^{+1}\qquad\searrow\\[-2mm] R\Gamma_x(F'|Y)\longleftarrow R\Gamma_x(F'), \end{array} \] compatible with the pairing (*) and the isomorphism (**), and giving rise to two exact sequences transposed of each other (4.7.15).

Remark 4.7.17. In the particular case where \(Y=\{x\}\), the pairing (4.7.1) is defined under the sole hypothesis that \(K_X\) be normalized at \(x\) (and not necessarily dualizing), i.e. such that \(R\Gamma_x(K_X)\simeq A\), (the isomorphism being given); the isomorphism (4.7.2) (which used biduality) then reduces to the identity. Since the pairing (4.7.14) is in any case perfect, we therefore see that local duality on \(X\) (in the form (4.2.2)') is equivalent to "global" duality on \(U=X-x\), i.e. to the fact that the pairing (4.7.1) \[R\Gamma(U,G)\times R\Gamma(U,G')[-1]\longrightarrow A\] is perfect, or again that the pairings \[H^i(U,G)\times H^{-i-1}(U,G')\longrightarrow A\] are perfect.

Suppose that we have on \(X\) a dualizing complex \(K_X\) normalized at \(x\).
