If \(X\) is excellent and \(U\) regular of dimension \(d\) (\(x\) may thus be an isolated singularity), it should be possible to check that \(K_U=K_X|U\) is canonically isomorphic to \((\mu_n)_U^{\otimes d}[2d]\) (one checks it easily in any case if \(X\) is the strict localization of a prescheme locally of finite type over a field of char. 0). Then local duality on \(X\) implies that we have, for every locally constant constructible \(A_U\)-module \(G\), perfect pairings: \[H^i(U,G)\times H^{2d-i-1}(U,G^\circ)\longrightarrow A,\] (where \(G^\circ=\underline{\operatorname{Hom}} (G,(\mu_n)_U^{\otimes d})\)), which correspond formally to Poincaré duality on a scheme of dimension \(2d-1\).

blocks.html: 5. Local duality on curves.

Theorem 5.1. Let \(X\) be a noetherian regular prescheme of dimension 1. We assume \(n\) prime to the residue characteristics of \(X\). Then the purity theorem is true on \(X\) (3.1.4) and the complex \(A_X\) is dualizing (1.7).

Proof: Suppose purity proved. Since \(X\) obviously satisfies condition (reg) (3.1.1), and the condition \((\mathrm{cdloc})_n\) is satisfied by virtue of (SGAA X 2.2), we conclude, by (3.2.1), that \(\dim.q\text{-}\operatorname{inj}(A_X)=2\). On the other hand, condition (ii) of (1.7) is satisfied by (3.3.1 b) (ii) (which was proved assuming purity). Hence, by virtue of (4.4), we are reduced to proving that \(A_X\) is dualizing when \(X\) is strictly local. But, to prove purity, which is a local question in the neighborhood of a closed point, we may equally assume \(X\) strictly local. Finally, we are reduced to proving the theorem when \(X\) is strictly local.

Suppose then \(X\) strictly local. Let \(x\) be its closed point, \(\pi=\operatorname{car}(k(x))\), \(\eta\) its generic point, \(U=X-x=\operatorname{Spec}(k(\eta))\). Let \(G=\operatorname{Gal}(k(\bar\eta)/k(\eta))\) be the fundamental group of \(U\). We know [CL Chap. IV § 2 Exer 2] that there is a canonical exact sequence \[\tag{5.1.1}1\longrightarrow P\longrightarrow G\longrightarrow H \longrightarrow1,\] where \[H=\prod_{\ell'\ne\pi}\mathbf Z_{\ell'}(1),\qquad \mathbf Z_\ell(1)=\varprojlim_\nu\mu_{\ell^\nu},\] and \(P\) is a pro-\(\pi\)-group. (Of course, \(H\) is isomorphic, though not canonically, to \(\prod_{\ell'\ne\pi}\mathbf Z_{\ell'}\).)

We know on the other hand (SGAA VIII 2) that the étale cohomology of \(U\) is interpreted as the Galois cohomology of \(G\), the sheaves (resp. constructible sheaves) of \(A_U\)-modules corresponding to the \(A\)-modules (resp. \(A\)-modules of finite type) on which \(G\) acts continuously.

That being so, the \(H_x^i(A_X)\) are determined by the exact sequence \[\tag{5.1.2}0\longrightarrow H_x^0(A_X)\longrightarrow H^0(X,A_X) \longrightarrow H^0(U,A_U)\longrightarrow H_x^1(A_X) \longrightarrow0,\] and the isomorphisms \[\tag{5.1.3}H_x^i(A_X)\simeq H^{i-1}(U,A_U)\quad\text{for }i\geq2.\] Now we trivially have \(H^0(X,A_X)=H^0(U,A_U)=A\), hence \(H_x^0(A_X)=H_x^1(A_X)=0\). On the other hand, for a (Galois) \(G\)-\(A\)-module \(M\), we have the Hochschild-Serre spectral sequence \[E_2^{pq}=H^p(H,H^q(P,M))\Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(G,M).\] Since \(n\) is prime to \(\pi\) and \(P\) is a pro-\(\pi\)-group, we have \[H^q(P,M)=0\quad\text{for }q\ne0,\] hence \(E_2^{pq}=0\) for \(q\ne0\), whence a canonical isomorphism \[\tag{5.1.4}H^p(G,M)\xrightarrow{\sim}H^p(H,M^P).\] One sees moreover, by an analogous argument, that \(H^p(H,N)\) is identified with \(H^p(\widehat{\mathbf Z}(1),N)\) when one makes the complementary factor of \(\widehat{\mathbf Z}(1)\) act trivially on \(N\). We thus finally have, with the preceding convention, a canonical isomorphism \[\tag{5.1.5}H^p(G,M)\simeq H^p(\widehat{\mathbf Z}(1),M^P).\] Now the Galois cohomology of \(\widehat{\mathbf Z}\) is well known (CG Chap. I p. 51): for a \(G\)-\(A\)-module \(N\), we have \[\tag{5.1.6} \begin{aligned} H^i(\widehat{\mathbf Z},N)&=0 &&\text{for }i\geq2,\\ H^0(\widehat{\mathbf Z},N)&=N^{\widehat{\mathbf Z}},\\ H^1(\widehat{\mathbf Z},N)&=N_{\widehat{\mathbf Z}}. \end{aligned} \]

From (5.1.3), (5.1.5), and (5.1.6) we deduce at once that \[H^i(U,A_U)=0\quad\text{for }i\geq2,\qquad H^1(U,A_U)\simeq A.\] This last isomorphism is not canonical, since it depends on the choice of an identification of \(\widehat{\mathbf Z}(1)\) with \(\widehat{\mathbf Z}\). But, using the Kummer exact sequence, one finds (SGAA XIX 1.3) a canonical isomorphism (given by the "local fundamental class") \[\tag{5.1.7}H^1(U,\mu_n)\simeq A.\] Purity is therefore proved.

Let us now prove that \(A_X\) is dualizing. By (4.7.17), the point is to show that, for every locally constant constructible \(A_U\)-module \(M\), the pairing \[\tag{5.1.8}H^i(U,M)\times H^{1-i}(U,\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(M,A_U)) \longrightarrow H^1(U,A_U)\simeq(\mu_n)_x^{\otimes-1}\] is a perfect duality. We are going to interpret this pairing in terms of Galois cohomology. We shall use the following elementary lemma:

Lemma 5.1.9. Denote by \(D=\operatorname{Hom}(-,A)\) the Pontrjagin dualizing functor on the category of \(A\)-modules of finite type. Let \(M\) be an \(A\)-module of finite type on which a group \(G\) acts \(A\)-linearly.

(i) We have a canonical isomorphism \[D(M^G)\simeq(DM)_G.\]

(ii) If \(G\) is a profinite group acting continuously on \(M\) and if \(G\) is of pro-order prime to \(n\), the canonical arrow \[M^G\longrightarrow M_G\] is an isomorphism.

Proof: We have by definition \[M^G=\varprojlim_{s\in G}\operatorname{Ker} (M\xrightarrow{s-1}M),\] whence \[ D(M^G)\simeq\varinjlim_{s\in G}\operatorname{Coker} (DM\xrightarrow{s-1}DM)\simeq(DM)_G, \] which proves (i).

Let us prove (ii). Let \(M'\) be the \(A\)-module defined by the exact sequence \[0\longrightarrow M'\longrightarrow M\longrightarrow M_G \longrightarrow0.\] Now we have \(H^1(G,M')=0\) because \(G\) is of pro-order prime to \(n\). The cohomology exact sequence therefore implies that the arrow \(M^G\longrightarrow M_G\) is an epimorphism. But, applying the functor \(D\) and taking (i) into account, we deduce that it is a monomorphism, which completes the proof.

Applying (5.1.9) to \(M\) and \(P\), we find that \(\operatorname{Hom}(M,A)^P\) is canonically identified with \(\operatorname{Hom}(M^P,A)\). If we choose an isomorphism of \(\widehat{\mathbf Z}(1)\) onto \(\widehat{\mathbf Z}\), the pairing (5.1.8) reads, taking (5.1.5) into account, \[\tag{5.1.8}'H^i(\widehat{\mathbf Z},M^P)\times H^{1-i}(\widehat{\mathbf Z},D(M^P)) \longrightarrow H^1(\widehat{\mathbf Z},A)\simeq A.\] It follows at once from (5.1.6) and (5.1.9(i)) that (5.1.8)' is a perfect pairing. This completes the proof of (5.1).
BIBLIOGRAPHY
[2] Hironaka, H. Resolution of singularities of an algebraic variety over a field of characteristic zero, Ann. of Math., vol. 79 (1964), p. 109.

[1] Abhyankar, S. Local uniformization on algebraic surfaces over ground fields of characteristic \(p\ne0\), Ann. of Math., vol. 63 (1956), p. 491–526, and Resolution of singularities of arithmetical surfaces, Arithmetical Algebraic Geometry, Harper & Row, New York (1965).
[H] Hartshorne, R. Residues and duality, Lecture Notes nº 20, Springer (1966).
[SGAA] Séminaire de Géométrie Algébrique de l'IHES, Cohomologie étale des schémas, by M. Artin and A. Grothendieck, 1963–64.
[CL] Serre, J.-P. Corps locaux, Hermann (1962).
[CG] Serre, J.-P. Cohomologie galoisienne, Lecture Notes nº 5, Springer (1964).
APPENDIX
by L. ILLUSIE

The preceding theory extends easily to base Rings more general than the constant Ring \(\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z\). We are going to indicate briefly how the theory reads for base Rings which are locally constant, killed by \(n>0\), and whose geometric stalks are noetherian rings. This is, moreover, only a provisional framework(*): sooner or later one will have to globalize, taking as "coefficients" on a prescheme \(X\) relative preschemes over the site \((X_{\mathrm{ét}},\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z)\).
1. Constructible sheaves

1.1. Let \(X\) be a prescheme, and let \(A_X\) be a sheaf of rings on \(X\) (for the étale topology), locally constant, whose geometric stalks are left noetherian rings. We denote by \(A^X\) the category of left \(A_X\)-Modules, and by \(D(A^X)\) (or \(D(X)\) if there is no risk of confusion) the corresponding derived category. The notion of a (left) constructible \(A_X\)-Module is defined as in (SGAA IX 2.3), by replacing the expression « locally constant with value of finite presentation » by « of finite presentation as an \(A_U\)-Module ».

We denote by \(D_c(X)\) the full subcategory of \(D(X)\) consisting of the complexes \(E\) such that \(\underline H^i(E)\) is a constructible \(A_X\)-Module for every \(i\). It is a triangulated subcategory of \(D(X)\).

1.2. Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D(X)\). We say (cf. Exp. II 3.11) that \(E\) is pseudo-coherent if, for every \(i\), \(\underline H^i(E)\) is an \(A_X\)-Module of finite presentation (or locally constant constructible if one prefers). We denote by \(D(X)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (or \(D_{\mathrm{coh}}(X)\)) the full subcategory of \(D(X)\) consisting of the pseudo-coherent objects. It follows from (SGAA IX 2.1) that it is a triangulated subcategory of \(D_c(X)\).

Proposition 1.3 (dévissage lemma). Let \(X\) be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated (resp. noetherian) prescheme, and let \(C\) be a strictly full subcategory of the category \(\operatorname{Cons}(X)\) of constructible left \(A_X\)-Modules. We assume \(C\) stable under extensions. The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(C=\operatorname{Cons}(X)\);

(ii) \(C\) contains the sheaves of the form \(i_!(F)\), where \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) is a subprescheme of finite presentation (resp. an integral subprescheme), and \(F\) is locally constant constructible on \(Y\).

If \(A_X\) is constant with value \(A\), a left noetherian ring annihilated by an integer \(n>0\), the preceding conditions are equivalent to:

(iii) \(C\) is stable under direct factors and contains the sheaves of the form \(f_*i_!(M_U)\), where \(i:U\longrightarrow Y\) is an open immersion (resp. an open subset of an integral prescheme), \(f:Y\longrightarrow X\) a finite morphism (resp. finite and such that the generic point of \(Y\) is separable over its image), and \(M_U\) a constant left \(A_U\)-Module whose value is a monogenic \(A\)-module annihilated by a prime divisor of \(n\).

Finally, if \(X\) is noetherian and satisfies the condition (reg) (I 3.1.1), condition (ii) (resp. (iii)) is equivalent to the analogous condition in which one assumes in addition that \(Y\) (resp. \(U\)) is regular.

Proof: The equivalence of conditions (i) and (ii) is proved as in the case where \(A_X\) is constant, see (SGAA IX 2.4 and 2.5). Suppose \(A_X\) constant with value \(A\) annihilated by \(n\), and let us prove that (iii) implies (ii). Let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Cons}(X)\), and let us show that \(E\in\operatorname{ob}C\). By the equivalence (i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (ii), we may assume that \(E=j_!(F)\), where \(j:Z\longrightarrow X\) is the immersion of a subprescheme of finite presentation (resp. an integral subprescheme), and \(F\) a locally constant constructible \(A_Z\)-Module, which we may even assume (after dévissing a little further) to be trivialized by an étale covering \(g:Z'\longrightarrow Z\) with group \(G\). The sheaf \(F\) is therefore defined by an \(A\)-module of finite type \(M\) on which \(G\) acts. If \(\ell\) is a prime divisor of \(n\), the \(\ell\)-torsion subgroup of \(M\) is a sub-\(G\)-\(A\)-module \(M_\ell\) of \(M\), and we have \[M=\bigoplus M_\ell,\] where \(\ell\) runs through the set of prime divisors of \(n\). In view of the stability of \(C\) under direct factors, we are thus reduced to the case where \(M\) is of \(\ell\)-torsion.

Let \(H\) be a \(\ell\)-Sylow subgroup of \(G\), and let \(h:U=Z'/H\longrightarrow Z\) be the intermediate covering corresponding to \(H\). The degree of \(h\) being prime to \(\ell\), the « trace method » (SGAA IX 5.1) shows that the adjunction arrow \[F\longrightarrow h_*h^*(F)\] is an isomorphism onto a direct factor, hence it suffices to prove that \(j_!h_*h^*(F)\in\operatorname{ob}C\). By virtue of the « Main Theorem » (EGA IV 8.12.6), there exists a commutative diagram \[ \begin{array}{ccc} U&\xrightarrow{i}&Y\\ \downarrow h&&\downarrow f\\ Z&\xrightarrow{j}&X \end{array} \] where \(i\) is an open immersion, and \(f\) a finite morphism. We have \[j_!h_*h^*(F)\simeq f_*i_!h^*(F).\] Now \(h^*(F)\) is defined by \(M\) regarded as an \(H\)-\(A\)-module. The implication (iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii) will therefore follow from the following lemma:

Lemma 1.3.1. Let \(A\) be a left noetherian ring, \(\ell\) a prime number, \(H\) a finite \(\ell\)-group, \(M\) a left \(H\)-\(A\)-module of finite type annihilated by \(\ell^\nu\) (\(\nu\geq1\)). There exists a finite filtration of \(M\) by sub-\(H\)-\(A\)-modules such that the successive quotients are monogenic \(A\)-modules annihilated by \(\ell\) and on which \(H\) acts trivially.

Proof: The exact sequence (of \(H\)-\(A\)-modules) \[0\longrightarrow\ell M\longrightarrow M\longrightarrow M/\ell M \longrightarrow0\] reduces us, by induction on \(\nu\), to the case where \(M\) is annihilated by \(\ell\). Let us show that in this case \(M\) admits a finite filtration by sub-\(H\)-\(A\)-modules such that \(H\) acts trivially on the successive quotients. Arguing by noetherian induction on the set of sub-\(H\)-\(A\)-modules of \(M\) possessing the stated property, we are reduced to proving that \(M\ne0\) implies \(M^H\ne0\). Now one checks trivially that the kernel of the augmentation \(\mathbf F_\ell[H]\longrightarrow\mathbf F_\ell\) is a nilpotent ideal, and Nakayama's lemma implies that every \(H\)-\(\mathbf F_\ell\)-module not reduced to 0 possesses a nonzero invariant vector, which proves our assertion. Hence \(M\) admits a finite filtration such that the successive quotients are \(A\)-modules of finite type annihilated by \(\ell\) and on which \(H\) acts trivially. By noetherian induction we can even arrange that the successive quotients be monogenic \(A\)-modules, which completes the proof of (1.3.1).
The last assertion of (1.3) is immediate and left to the reader.
2. Quasi-injective dimension.

The notation being that of (1.1), we define as in (I 1.1 and 1.4) the notions of a quasi-injective \(A_X\)-Module, and of the quasi-injective dimension of an object of \(D(X)\). Propositions (I 1.3, 1.5 and 1.6) extend without change.

It is sometimes convenient to introduce a notion of pointwise quasi-injective dimension. Let \(x\in X\), and let \(K\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\). We say that \(K\) is of quasi-injective dimension \(\leq N\) at \(\bar x\) if, for every constructible \(A_X\)-Module \(F\), we have \[\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^i(F,K)_{\bar x}=0\quad\text{for }i>N.\] We call the quasi-injective dimension of \(K\) at \(\bar x\), and we denote by \(\dim.q.\operatorname{inj}_{\bar x}(K)\), the infimum of the \(N\) satisfying the preceding condition. Of course, we have \[\dim.q.\operatorname{inj}(K)= \sup_{\bar x}\dim.q.\operatorname{inj}_{\bar x}(K),\] where \(\bar x\) runs through the set of geometric points of \(X\).

It is interesting to compare the preceding notion with the notion of strict topological dimension introduced by Verdier in [1]. We say that \(K\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\) is of strict topological dimension \(\leq N\) at a geometric point \(\bar x\) of \(X\) if for every subprescheme \(Y\) of \(X\), we have \[\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^i(A_Y,K)_{\bar x} =\underline H_Y^i(K)_{\bar x}=0\quad\text{for }i>N.\] We call the strict topological dimension of \(K\) at \(\bar x\), and we denote by \(\dimstop_{\bar x}(K)\), the infimum of the \(N\) satisfying the preceding condition.

While the quasi-injective dimension depends a priori on the base Ring, the strict topological dimension depends only on the underlying complex of abelian sheaves. We obviously have: \[\dimstop_{\bar x}(K)\leq\dim.q.\operatorname{inj}_{\bar x}(K).\] We shall see further below examples where equality holds.

pages.title: 3. Dualizing complexes: definition and formal properties.

blocks.html: 3. Dualizing complexes: definition and formal properties.

Let us keep the preceding notation, but assume \(A_X\) commutative. The notions of bidualizing pair and of dualizing complex are defined as in (I 1.7). The statements (I 1.9, 1.11, 1.14) are valid without modification. The statements (I 1.12, 1.13, 1.15) are also valid, but on condition that we assume \(A\) annihilated by an integer \(n>0\) prime to the residue characteristics of \(Y\). Finally, the uniqueness theorem (I 2.1) is valid provided we assume \(\operatorname{Spec}(A_{\bar x})\) connected for every geometric point \(\bar x\) of \(X\).
4. Absolute purity

Let us return to the situation of absolute cohomological purity (I 3.1.4). Let \(X\) be a regular locally noetherian prescheme, and let \(n>0\) be an integer prime to the residue characteristics of \(X\). Let \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) be a regular subprescheme of codimension \(d>0\) at every point. To say that the pair \((Y,X)\) satisfies absolute cohomological purity means, let us recall, that the « local fundamental class » homomorphism (SGA \(4^{1/2}\) Cycle 2.2)
\[\tag{4.1}(\mu_n)_Y^{\otimes-d}[-2d]\longrightarrow Ri^!(\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z)_X\] is an isomorphism.

Let us now give ourselves on \(X\) a sheaf of rings \(A_X\) annihilated by \(n\). Set for a moment \(G=(\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z)_X\). Then, for every \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(A_X)\), we can define a canonical arrow of \(D^+(A_Y)\): \[\tag{4.2}i^*(F)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes_{\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z}} Ri^!(G)\longrightarrow Ri^!\bigl(F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes_{\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z}}G\bigr).\] Indeed, it amounts to the same, by duality (SGAA XVIII 3.1.6), to define an arrow \[ i_!\bigl(i^*(F)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes_{\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z}} Ri^!(G)\bigr)\longrightarrow F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes_{\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z}}G; \] for which we shall choose the composite of the Künneth arrow (SGAA XVII 5.4.2.2) \[ i_!\bigl(i^*(F)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes_{\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z}} Ri^!(G)\bigr)\longrightarrow F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes_{\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z}}i_!Ri^!(G), \] and of the arrow deduced from the trace homomorphism (SGAA XVIII 3.1.6) \[ F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes_{\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z}}i_!Ri^!(G) \longrightarrow F\mathbin{\overset L\otimes_{\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z}}G. \]

Note that in the definition of (4.2) we have only used the fact that \(G\) and \(Ri^!(G)\) were of finite tor-dimension over \(\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z\). Suppose on the other hand that the functor \(i^!\) is of finite cohomological dimension on the category of \((\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z)_X\)-Modules: this is the case for example if \(X\) is of finite type over a field (SGAA XIV 3.1), or more generally if \(X\) is excellent of equal characteristics (SGAA XIX 6.1) when resolution of singularities is available, or again if \(X\) is of finite type over \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbf Z)\) (SGAA X 6). Then, the arrow (4.2) is defined for every \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D(A_X)\).

Composing (4.1) and (4.2), we thus have, for \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(A_X)\), a canonical arrow \[\tag{4.3}i^*(F)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes_{\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z}} (\mu_n)_Y^{\otimes-d}[-2d]\longrightarrow Ri^!(F).\]

Suppose that \(A_X\) is locally constant, and let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(A_X)\) be such that, for every \(i\), \(\underline H^i(F)\) is a locally constant \(A_X\)-Module. Then, if \(i^!\) is of finite cohomological dimension, the arrow (4.2) (and consequently the arrow (4.3) if \((Y,X)\) satisfies purity) is an isomorphism.

Indeed, by dévissage, we first reduce to the case where \(F\) is concentrated in degree 0. The question being local, we may assume \(X\) affine, and \(A_X\) and \(F\) constant. Resolving \(F\) on the left by free \(A_X\)-Modules, and applying the hypothesis that \(i^!\) is of finite cohomological dimension, we reduce to the case where \(F\) is free. By an immediate passage to the limit, we reduce to \(F\) free of finite type, and finally to \(F=A_X\). It therefore suffices to prove the assertion in the case \(A_X=(\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z)_X\). But the preceding dévissage reduces us to \(F=(\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z)_X\), and in that case the arrow (4.2) reduces to the identity.

Definition 4.4. The Ring \(A_X\) being given (locally constant, annihilated by \(n\)), we shall say that the pair \((Y,X)\) satisfies cohomological purity in the strong sense if, for every \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(A_X)\) such that the \(\underline H^i(F)\) are locally constant, the canonical arrow (4.3) is an isomorphism.
We shall say that the purity theorem is true in the strong sense on \(X\) if, locally (for the étale topology), every pair \((Y,X)\) as above satisfies purity in the strong sense.

By what we have seen, in order for \((Y,X)\) to satisfy purity in the strong sense, it therefore suffices that \((Y,X)\) satisfy purity in the ordinary sense (I 3.1.) and that the functor \(i^!\) be of finite cohomological dimension.
5. Bounding the quasi-injective dimension.

Proposition 5.1. Let \(X\) be a regular locally noetherian prescheme, equipped with a locally constant sheaf of rings \(A_X\), annihilated by an integer \(n>0\) prime to the residue characteristics of \(X\), and whose geometric stalks are left noetherian rings. We assume that \(X\) satisfies the conditions (reg) (I 3.1.1) and \((\mathrm{cdloc})_n\) (I 3.1.3), and that the purity theorem in the strong sense is true on \(X\) (4.4) (conditions which are all satisfied if \(X\) is smooth over a perfect field, or if \(X\) is excellent of characteristic zero).

Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(A_X)\) be such that the \(\underline H^i(F)\) are locally constant, and let \(x\) be a point of \(X\) such that we have \(F_{\bar x}\ne0\) and \(\dim.\operatorname{inj}_{A_{\bar x}}(F_{\bar x})<+\infty\). Under these conditions we have: \[ \dim.q.\operatorname{inj}_{\bar x}(F) =2\dim(\mathcal O_{X,x}) +\dim.\operatorname{inj}(F_{\bar x}), \] with the notation of nº 2.

Proof. We need only repeat step by step the proof of (I 3.2.1). We shall first show the inequality \[ \dim.q.\operatorname{inj}_{\bar x}(F) \leq2\dim(\mathcal O_{X,x})+\dim.\operatorname{inj}(F_{\bar x})=N, \] i.e. the relation \[\tag{*}\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^i(E,F)_{\bar x}=0 \quad\text{for }i>N\text{ and every constructible }A_X\text{-Module }E. \] The question being local, we may, by dévissage (1.3), restrict ourselves to verifying (*) for \(E=i_!(M)\), where \(i:Y\longrightarrow X\) is an integral regular subprescheme closed in an open subset \(D(f)\) (\(f\in\Gamma(X,\mathcal O_X)\)), of codimension \(d\) at every point of \(D(f)\), and \(M\) a locally constant constructible \(A_Y\)-Module (\(A_Y=i^*A_X\)).

Now we have the duality isomorphism (SGAA XVIII) \[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(i_!M,F)\simeq Ri_*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(M,Ri^!F). \] By the purity theorem, we have on the other hand the isomorphism (4.3) \[ i^*(F)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes_{\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z}} (\mu_n)_Y^{\otimes-d}[-2d]\xrightarrow{\sim}Ri^!(F). \] Let \(\bar y\) be a geometric point of \(Y\). Since \(M\) is locally constant constructible, we have (canonically) \[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(M,Ri^!F)_{\bar y}\simeq R\operatorname{Hom}(M_{\bar y},(Ri^!F)_{\bar y}), \] hence, thanks to (4.3), \[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(M,Ri^!F)_{\bar y}\simeq R\operatorname{Hom}(M_{\bar y},F_{\bar y})[-2d]. \] We may on the other hand assume that we have placed ourselves in a connected neighborhood of \(x\). We then have \[ F_{\bar x}\simeq F_{\bar y},\qquad A_{\bar x}\simeq A_{\bar y}, \] since \(A_X\) and the \(\underline H^i(F)\) are locally constant, whence \[ \underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^i(M_{\bar y},F_{\bar y})=0 \quad\text{for }i>\dim.\operatorname{inj}(F_{\bar x}), \] and finally \[ \underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^q(M,Ri^!F)=0 \quad\text{for }q>2d+\dim.\operatorname{inj}(F_{\bar x}). \]

Set \(G=R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(M,Ri^!F)\), and let us now compute \(Ri_*(G)_{\bar x}\). For this, we introduce, as in (I 3.2.1), the schematic closure \(Z\) of \(Y\) in \(X\), whence a cartesian diagram of immersions \[ \begin{array}{ccc} Y&\longrightarrow&D(f)\\ \downarrow j&&\downarrow\\ Z&\xrightarrow{k}&X. \end{array} \] We therefore have \(Ri_*(G)\simeq k_*Rj_*(G)\). We assume of course that \(x\in Z\), otherwise we would trivially have \(Ri_*(G)_{\bar x}=0\). Let us then introduce the strict localization \(\bar Z=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{Z,\bar x})\) of \(Z\) at \(\bar x\), and denote by \(\bar f\) the image of \(f\) in \(\mathcal O_{Z,\bar x}\), \(\bar Y=\bar Z\times_ZY=\bar Z\cap D(f)\), \(\bar G\) the inverse image of \(G\) on \(\bar Y\). By (SGAA VIII 5.2), we have \[ Ri_*(G)_{\bar x}=Rj_*(G)_{\bar x}\simeq R\Gamma(\bar Y,\bar G). \] The condition \((\mathrm{cdloc})_n\) therefore implies, via a spectral sequence argument, that we have \[ R^pi_*(G)_{\bar x}=0\quad\text{for } p>2d+\dim.\operatorname{inj}(F_{\bar x}) +\dim(\mathcal O_{Z,x}). \] But we have (cf. I 3.2.1, proof) \[ \dim(\mathcal O_{Z,x})=\dim(\mathcal O_{X,x})-d\geq0, \] hence \[ R^pi_*(G)_{\bar x}=0\quad\text{for } p>d+\dim(\mathcal O_{X,x}) +\dim.\operatorname{inj}(F_{\bar x}), \] hence a fortiori for \(p>2\dim(\mathcal O_{X,x})+\dim.\operatorname{inj}(F_{\bar x})\), which proves (*).

It remains to prove that the bound (*) is the best possible. Set \(f=\dim.\operatorname{inj}(F_{\bar x})\), and let \(M'\) be a \(A_{\bar x}\)-Module of finite type such that \[\operatorname{Ext}^f(M',F_{\bar x})\ne0.\] Let \(Z\) be the integral closed subprescheme of \(X\) which is the closure of \(x\), and let \(Y=U\cap Z\) be a regular open subset of \(Z\) containing \(x\) and of codimension \(d=\dim(\mathcal O_{X,x})\) at every point. After shrinking \(Y\) if necessary, there exists a locally constant constructible \(A_Y\)-Module \(M\) such that \(M_{\bar x}=M'\). Applying the purity theorem, we then find \[ \underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^{2d+f}(M,F|U)_{\bar x} \simeq\operatorname{Ext}^f(M',F_{\bar x})\ne0, \] which completes the proof.
Note that if \(F\) is a \(A_X\)-Module such that \(F_{\bar x}\) is injective, we may, in the last part of the proof, choose \(M=A_Y\); we therefore obtain:

Corollary 5.1.1. Under the hypotheses of (5.1), suppose that \(F\) is a locally constant \(A_X\)-Module such that \(F_{\bar x}\) is injective. Then we have \[ \dim.q.\operatorname{inj}_{\bar x}(F) =\dimstop_{\bar x}(F)=2\dim(\mathcal O_{X,x}), \] with the notation of nº 2.

Remarks 5.1.2. a) With the notation of (5.1), if \(F_{\bar x}=0\), then \(F=0\) in a neighborhood of \(x\), hence \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(E,F)_{\bar x}=0\) for every \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(X)\).

b) If \(\dim.\operatorname{inj}(F_{\bar x})=+\infty\), then \(\dim.q.\operatorname{inj}_{\bar x}(F)=+\infty\); indeed, for every \(p\), there exists a \(A_{\bar x}\)-Module of finite type \(M'\) such that \(\operatorname{Ext}^p(M',F_{\bar x})\ne0\), hence a constructible \(A_X\)-Module \(M\), locally constant in a neighborhood of \(x\), such that \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^p(M,F)_{\bar x}\ne0\).
6. Constructibility of \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}\).

The equivalence of the conditions a) of (I 3.3.1) is still valid when we replace the Ring \((\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z)_X\) by a commutative, locally constant Ring whose geometric stalks are noetherian rings: there is nothing to change in the proof.

The preceding conditions are satisfied when resolution of singularities and purity in the strong sense (4.4) are available, in particular if \(X\) is of dimension \(\leq1\), or if \(X\) is excellent of characteristic zero, or locally of finite type over a field and of dimension \(\leq2\).
7. Existence of dualizing complexes.
In this section, \(X\) denotes a prescheme equipped with a sheaf of rings \(A_X\) which is commutative, locally constant, and whose geometric stalks are noetherian rings.

Lemma 7.1. Let \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-_{\mathrm{coh}}(X)\), \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D^+_{\mathrm{coh}}(X)\); then \[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,G) \in D^+_{\mathrm{coh}}(X) \] (notation of (1.2)).
Proof: This is standard. We reduce, by a spectral sequence, to \(F\) and \(G\) concentrated in degree 0, and then proceed as in (EGA \(0_{\mathrm{III}}\) 12.3.3).

Lemma 7.2. Let \(g:X'\longrightarrow X\) be a morphism of preschemes, and equip \(X'\) with the sheaf of rings \(A_{X'}=g^*(A_X)\). Then, for \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^-_{\mathrm{coh}}(X)\), \(G\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(X)\), the canonical arrow \[ g^*R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,G)\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(g^*F,g^*G) \] is an isomorphism.
Proof: This is likewise standard: we reduce, by dévissage, to \(F=A_X\) (cf. EGA \(0_{\mathrm{III}}\) 12.3.5).
Remark 7.2.1. The preceding lemmas are special cases of more general statements which appear in (SGA 6 I).

Lemma 7.3. Suppose \(X\) quasi-compact, and let \(K\in\operatorname{ob}D^+_{\mathrm{coh}}(X)\) be such that, for every geometric point \(\bar x\) of \(X\), \(K_{\bar x}\) is a dualizing complex in the sense of [H] V 2.1. Then, for every \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D^b_{\mathrm{coh}}(X)\), we have \[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,K) \in\operatorname{ob}D^b_{\mathrm{coh}}(X), \] and \(F\) is reflexive relative to \(K\).

Proof: We may, of course, restrict ourselves to carrying out the verification for \(F\) concentrated in degree 0. By (7.1), we have \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,K) \in\operatorname{ob}D^+_{\mathrm{coh}}(X)\). Let us prove that we have \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,K) \in D^b_{\mathrm{coh}}(X)\). Let \(\bar x\) be a geometric point of \(X\). By (7.2), we have \[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,K)_{\bar x} \xrightarrow{\sim}R\operatorname{Hom}(F_{\bar x},K_{\bar x}). \] If we set \(\dim.\operatorname{inj}(K_{\bar x})=N(\bar x)\), we therefore have \[ \underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^i(F,K)_{\bar x}=0 \quad\text{for }i>N(\bar x). \] Since the \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^i(F,K)\) are locally constant, there then exists an open neighborhood \(U\) of \(x\) such that \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^i(F,K)|U=0\) for \(i>N(\bar x)\), and we conclude thanks to the quasi-compactness hypothesis on \(X\).

It remains to verify that the canonical arrow \[ F\longrightarrow R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}} (R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,K),K) \] is an isomorphism. For this it suffices to place ourselves at a geometric point \(\bar x\) of \(X\). In the commutative diagram \[ \begin{array}{ccc} F_{\bar x}&\longrightarrow& R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}} (R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,K),K)_{\bar x}\\ \Vert&&\downarrow\\ F_{\bar x}&\longrightarrow& R\operatorname{Hom} (R\operatorname{Hom}(F_{\bar x},K_{\bar x}),K_{\bar x}), \end{array} \] the right-hand vertical arrow is an isomorphism by (7.2) and the bottom horizontal arrow is an isomorphism because \(K_{\bar x}\) is dualizing, hence the upper horizontal arrow is an isomorphism, q.e.d.

Lemma 7.4. We assume that \(X\) is a regular noetherian prescheme, that \(A_X\) is annihilated by an integer \(n>0\) prime to the residue characteristics of \(X\), and that the purity theorem in the strong sense (4.4) is true on \(X\). Let \(D\) be a divisor with normal crossings on \(X\) (I 3.1.5 a)), and denote by \(j:Y=V(D)\longrightarrow X\) and \(i:X-Y=U\longrightarrow X\) the canonical inclusions. Let \(K\in\operatorname{ob}D^+_{\mathrm{coh}}(X)\) be such that, for every geometric point \(\bar x\) of \(X\), \(K_{\bar x}\) is a dualizing complex for the ring \(A_{\bar x}\) (in the sense of [H] V 2.1). Then, for every \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^b_{\mathrm{coh}}(X)\), we have \[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(i_!i^*(E),K) \in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X), \] and the pair \((i_!i^*(E),K)\) is bidualizing.

Proof: We may assume \(E\) concentrated in degree 0, i.e. a locally constant constructible \(A_X\)-Module. On the other hand, the question being local on \(X\) for the étale topology, we may assume that
(a) \(A_X\) is constant with value \(A\), and \(E\) is constant with value denoted \(E\) by abuse of language, say \(E=E_X\);
(b) \(D\) has strict normal crossings, say \(D=\sum_{i\in I}\operatorname{div}(s_i)\), where \(s_i\in\Gamma(X,\mathcal O_X)\).

Let us argue by induction on \(p=\operatorname{card}(I)\). For \(p=0\), the assertions are a consequence of (7.3). Suppose that \(I=\{1,\ldots,p\}\), set \(Y_p=V(s_p)\), \(Y'=V(\sum_{1\leq i\leq p-1}\operatorname{div}(s_i))\), \(U'=X-Y'\), whence a cartesian diagram of immersions \[ \begin{array}{ccccc} \varnothing&\longrightarrow&U'\cap Y_p&\xrightarrow{i'_p}&Y_p\\ \downarrow&&\downarrow j'_p&&\downarrow j_p\\ U&\longrightarrow&U'&\xrightarrow{i'}&X. \end{array} \] Consider the exact sequence \[ 0\longrightarrow E_{U,X}\longrightarrow E_{U',X} \longrightarrow E_{U'\cap Y_p,X}\longrightarrow0. \] By the induction hypothesis, the theorem is true for the pair \((E_{U',X},K)\). Since, for fixed \(K\), the set of \(F\) such that \(R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,K)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\) and such that \((F,K)\) is bidualizing forms a triangulated subcategory of \(D_c(X)\), we are reduced to proving the theorem for the pair \((E_{U'\cap Y_p,X},K)\). But we have \[ E_{U'\cap Y_p,X}=j_{p*}\,i'_{p!}(E_{U'\cap Y_p}), \] and, by (I 1.13), it amounts to the same to prove the theorem for the pair \[ \bigl(i'_{p!}(E_{U'\cap Y_p}),Rj_p^!(K)\bigr) \] in \(Y_p\). But, by the purity theorem, we have (4.3) \[ Rj_p^!(K)\simeq j_p^*(K)\mathbin{\overset L\otimes_{\mathbf Z/n\mathbf Z}} (\mu_n)_{Y_p}^{\otimes-1}[-2]. \] Hence \(Rj_p^!(K)\in\operatorname{ob}D^+_{\mathrm{coh}}(Y_p)\) and, for every geometric point \(\bar y\) of \(Y_p\), \((Rj_p^!K)_{\bar y}\) is dualizing, and consequently we conclude thanks to the induction hypothesis.

Theorem 7.5. We make the following hypotheses: \(X\) is a regular noetherian prescheme; \(A_X\) is annihilated by an integer \(n>0\) prime to the residue characteristics of \(X\); \(X\) is strongly desingularizable (I 3.1.5), and satisfies the conditions (reg) (I 3.1.1) and \((\mathrm{cdloc})_n\) (I 3.1.3); the purity theorem in the strong sense (4.4) is true on every prescheme smooth over \(X\) (conditions satisfied if \(X\) is excellent of characteristic zero, or of dimension \(\leq2\) and smooth over a perfect field).

Then, if \(K\in\operatorname{ob}D^+_{\mathrm{coh}}(X)\) is such that \(K_{\bar x}\) is dualizing (in the sense of [H] V 2.1) for every geometric point \(\bar x\) of \(X\), \(K\) is dualizing.

Proof. We must show that \(K\) satisfies the conditions (i) to (iii) of (I 1.7). For every geometric point \(\bar x\) of \(X\), \(K_{\bar x}\) is of finite injective dimension, and, since \(K\in\operatorname{ob}D^+_{\mathrm{coh}}(X)\), the function \[ x\longmapsto\dim.\operatorname{inj}(K_{\bar x}) \] is locally constant, hence bounded since \(X\) is noetherian. It then follows from (5.1) that \(K\) is of finite quasi-injective dimension.
By what we saw in nº 6, condition (ii) of loc. cit. is likewise satisfied.

It remains to prove condition (iii), i.e. that every \(F\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X)\) is reflexive relative to \(K\). By dévissage, we may assume \(F\) concentrated in degree 0. On the other hand, the question being local on \(X\) for the étale topology, we may assume \(A_X\) constant with value \(A\). The dévissage (1.3) then reduces us to \(F=f_*i_!(E_U)\), where \(f:Y\longrightarrow X\) is a finite morphism, with \(Y\) integral with generic point separable over its image, \(i:U\longrightarrow Y\) a regular open subset \(\ne\varnothing\), and \(E_U\) a constant \(A_U\)-Module with value \(E\), an \(A\)-module of finite type. Since \(X\) is strongly desingularizable, there exists (I 3.1.5) a projective and birational morphism \(h:Y'\longrightarrow Y\) such that \(Y'\) is regular, that \(h\) induces an isomorphism \(h^{-1}(U)\longrightarrow U\), and that \(h^{-1}(U)\) is the complement of a divisor with normal crossings in \(Y'\). Identifying \(h^{-1}(U)\) with \(U\) by \(h\), we therefore have a commutative diagram \[ \begin{array}{ccccc} &&Y'&&\\[-2mm] &{}^{i'}\nearrow&\downarrow h&\searrow^{g}&\\[-2mm] U&\xrightarrow{i}&Y&\xrightarrow{f}&X. \end{array} \] Consequently we have \[ f_*i_!(E_U)\simeq f_*Rh_*i'_!(E_U) \simeq Rg_*i'_!(E_U)\simeq Rg_*i'_!i'^*(E_{Y'}). \] By virtue of (I 1.13), it suffices to prove biduality for the pair \[ \bigl(i'_!i'^*(E_{Y'}),Rg^!(K)\bigr). \] But, embedding \(Y'\) locally in a prescheme \(X'\) smooth over \(X\) and applying purity in the strong sense on \(X'\) (4.4), we find that \(Rg^!(K)\) is locally isomorphic (up to translation of degrees) to \(g^*(K)\), hence that \(Rg^!(K)\in\operatorname{ob}D^+_{\mathrm{coh}}(Y')\) and that \(Rg^!(K)_{\bar x}\) is dualizing for every geometric point \(\bar x\) of \(Y'\). It then suffices to apply (7.4).

The preceding proof shows moreover that we have \[ R\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(F,K)\in\operatorname{ob}D_c^b(X) \] (granting the finiteness theorem for a proper morphism (SGAA XIV)), which will relieve the reader who might not have had confidence in the discourse of nº 6.
This completes the proof.
